items.title: Cursor just beat EVERYONE.
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: The first thing I want to talk about today is how the new cursor model is just quietly best coding model on the planet. Cursor released Composer 2.5, the next iteration of their homegrown coding models, the workhorse model, the model that they're probably all hoping everybody moves to because that's the one that they are having the highest margins on because it's their own model and built for coding.

items.summary: This shook me when I first read it and I couldn't quite figure out why and I thought about it and now I want to explain exactly why I think this is such a big deal. Andre Carpathy, if you're not familiar, is one of the most influential people in artificial intelligence.
items.transcriptSummary: This shook me when I first read it and I couldn't quite figure out why and I thought about it and now I want to explain exactly why I think this is such a big deal. Andre Carpathy, if you're not familiar, is one of the most influential people in artificial intelligence.

transcript.text: I've joined Anthropic. This shook me when I first read it and I couldn't quite figure out why and I thought about it and now I want to explain exactly why I think this is such a big deal. Andre Carpathy, if you're not familiar, is one of the most influential people in artificial intelligence. He invented self-driving at Tesla. He was an open AI co-founder which makes this entire story even more wild because now he's joining anthropic. Also one of the best educators in artificial intelligence able to break down extremely complex topics in ways that almost anybody can learn it. A lot of people thought this was quite shocking because look it has 24 million views on basically what amounts to a job update. This is actually a sign of the times. The AI labs have become almost religions and based on his decision to join Anthropic, we have learned a lot about his view of the future and it's not positive. I think the next few years at the frontier of LLM will be especially formative. I am very excited to join the team here and get back to R&D research and development. I remain deeply passionate about education and plan to resume my work on it in time. On multiple measures, Anthropic is winning the AI race. They now have the most revenue. They seemingly have the strongest culture whether you agree with the culture or not. And we're going to get into that. They also seem to be attracting, as exampled by Karpathy, the best people in the world to join them. And maybe that's another reflection of their culture. But what is their culture and how does it compare to open AI? It just seems like we're in a complete era of dogma. when you join a company you are planting a flag and saying this is what I believe and so by Karpathy joining anthropic he's basically validating a lot of what anthropic has been saying and if you don't remember what anthropic has been saying it's quite pessimistic about the future for example the founder of anthropic Daario Amodai spent last year warning of an AI white collar bloodbath he also leads the company that believes artificial intelligence could be conscious and that they believe it has the highest likelihood of becoming sentient and not in the good way, in the Terminator way. And also that these models are dangerous and that open source was a bad idea and also that they were the only company that was responsible enough or thoughtful enough to actually have the models and it just seems like Karpathy has joined the empire. by the way, completely wild that he decided to drop this news during Google IO's keynote. The point of this video is it just seems like pessimism is taking over. And so although Andre Karpathy didn't directly say it, by joining Anthropic, he is effectively saying Anthropic is the only company that he believes has a direct line of sight and the right set of ingredients to succeed with artificial intelligence. And I also had a sense of loss as I said that we lost this independent voice, this researcher who was also able to educate so many people about artificial intelligence. But also maybe he just believed this stuff. Maybe he believed that there really wasn't a bright future. It made me sad to realize that. I think about when Peter Steinberger joined Open AI. Peter Steinberger the creator of Open Claw. I had a similar feeling at first. I felt a loss. There was this absolute genius who was creating something new and innovative and independent and open-source. And when he joined OpenAI, I felt we lost a part of that. And I'm feeling that again here. And it just seems like there are two and a half companies that are just taking all of the oxygen out of the room. All of the points of view, all of the perspectives are just getting sucked into basically two and a half companies. And which ones are those? Of course, Open AI, we have Anthropic, and the half is X AI. They have strong perspectives. They just don't quite have that frontier model yet. One of the most interesting parts of this story is the fact that Andre Karpathy is an OpenAI co-founder. He left a while ago, but he was one of the original people on the OpenAI team. He also was on Elon's team at Tesla. But the interesting part now is he's joining Anthropic, and that is, at least from my perspective, a big slap in the face to Sam Alman and the Open AI culture. He didn't believe in them enough to go with them, even though he started there. And that is so wild to me. There is just something going on at Anthropic that pulls the best people in and keeps them there. Not a single Anthropic founder has left in in all the years that they've been operating. There is something to be said about that. And so, let's talk about the different perspectives of these companies. I think we all know Anthropic. I've already talked about it. Very pessimistic. They believe they're the only ones holier than thou that can create the right kind of AI in the future. Then we have open AI and they believe AI is more of a tool and that it is going to create abundance and that there is going to be this a bit painful transition period but mostly they have an optimistic view of the future. And then we have XAI and Elon Musk and they are pretty explicitly optimistic about the future redoing their company's mission and Tesla's mission. Even though XAI and Tesla are separate companies, they're basically the same because it's all Elon that there's going to be amazing abundance in the future and we're going to have not just universal basic income but universal high income. This is these are all words that Elon said directly. And so imagine you're one of the best researchers on the planet. You could go work anywhere. You can command compensation of any amount. And really to Cararpathy, it doesn't even matter. The money doesn't matter. He's already a billionaire most likely, right? Cuz he's a co-founder of OpenAI. His decision to join Anthropic is extremely telling. It's either he believes that they are the only company that is able to create artificial intelligence in a safe way or maybe just at all. He also believes in their world view. Job loss is going to be very real and very impactful. And then maybe he just saw something internally. He's technical through and through. He's a researcher at heart. And so he might have just seen something inside of Anthropic that got him so excited that he wanted to go and lend his expertise to the company to help them drive towards that future. Now typically people in tech are motivated by two things obviously money but the mission and I know it sounds corny to a lot of people but the mission of these companies is generally quite important when you can go work anywhere work on anything the actual problem you're solving and the reason why you are solving that problem is incredibly important and so again this is quite telling why carpathy joined anthropic. And look, this is something I think about quite a bit. It seems like pessimism, negativity towards AI has completely taken over the narrative. I mean, look at this Pew Research study. Americans continue to be wary of AI's impact on daily life. 50% of Americans are more concerned than excited about the increased use of AI in daily life. But it's clear a ton of people are concerned and it has actually been increasing especially in 2023 which is when chatbt was released and it has also become politicized. Politics especially in the US are completely polarized. There is no middle ground. There is no gray area. You're either for something or you're against it and you are vehemently so. Of course because AI is the biggest thing right now. It has become politicized. Whether you're talking about Bernie Sanders having a conversation with Claude on which is just a phone on the other side of the table, which is like the most absurd image ever. Let's not forget this. When Bernie Sanders decided to interview Claude and it was on this this stand with I don't know. It's just such like a crazy image, right? Are we are we seeing this? I mean, the fact that he's interviewing Claude and he thought this was a legitimate interview is the craziest thing ever. You know, whatever you think about Bernie Sanders, fine. But it has become completely politicized. Bernie Sanders is talking about pausing AI completely. AOC is talking about pausing AI completely. And just so you don't think I'm completely favoring one side, the Trump administration with the Department of War went to war with anthropic. And so, of course, because it has become so politicized, it is binary in that it's either extremely good or extremely bad. Just admitting something from my channel. I have historically been extremely optimistic about artificial intelligence. Maybe to the point where some people saw me as naive. And we actually had meetings as a team to think through that. And I actually think that was quite valid feedback. If you're optimistic about everything, your opinion doesn't carry as much weight. And so I try to be critical when it is warranted. But the general sentiment around AI right now is just seemingly pure pessimism outside of a small bubble of AI insiders. Even within that bubble, anthropics stands alone as a paragon of pessimism. Is paragon the right word? By the way, this isn't new. The entire social media apparatus is based on optimizing for fear and anger. Facebook and Meta kind of knew that for a while and they didn't do anything about it and this was especially harmful to kids. They actually had to pay a lot of money because of that. Just a few months ago, New Mexico jury says meta harms children's mental health and safety. So, this is nothing new. The social media algorithms are optimizing for one thing and that is engagement time on site because that means they get to serve more ads. And of course, human nature is attracted to fear and anger. Those are the easy emotions to gravitate towards. And we're seeing the same thing now with AI. Politicians want to win races. So, they point to the big bad boogeyman that is AI. AI data centers are going to drink up all the water. They're going to take all your jobs. It doesn't really matter what it is. It's just AI bad. It's certainly not all sunshine and rainbows. It's somewhere in the middle. Think about 24-hour news built around keeping you engaged and keeping you watching for as long as possible. So, of course, them as well, optimizing for fear and anger. If all they did was tell you that everything is good and you should feel optimistic, you'd probably turn the TV off and go outside and touch grass. But instead, they have to draw you in with that fear and anger. And that's what I am deeply concerned about because AI has the potential to have truly a positive impact on the world. And if we see it as really a deeply negative thing or a deeply polarizing thing, then we're going to have a large portion of the population fearful of it, which is bad in itself. But they're going to be more willing to enact policy to stop AI or to slow down AI. And that actually puts us at risk because I guarantee other countries are not going to be slowing down when it comes to AI. And AI is one of the most powerful technologies and innovations ever created. So we do want it to be ours. And by the way, I'm not saying that the AI industry is not to blame for some of this pessimism. AI companies are doing themselves no favors when it comes to the perception of artificial intelligence. And a great example is the ex Google CEO Eric Schmidt getting absolutely booed at a commencement speech where he conveys how important AI will be to those graduating students. >> And it would this time it was the architects of artificial intelligence. Interesting. Every person and every relationship you have. I know what many of you are feeling about that. I can hear you. There is a fear. We do not know. We do not know. And if you if you'd let me make this point, please. If you don't care about science, that's okay because AI is going to touch everything else as well. Whatever path you choose, AI will become part of how work is done. If you have a pro problem in the world you want to solve, you can now assemble a team of AI agents to help you with the parts that you could never accomplish on your own. Let me give you some advice. First, find a way to say yes and thank you very much and good night. >> And Lulu Changy actually had a great take. And if you're not familiar with who she is, she's basically a PR comm's master. Question is not whether AI will shape the world. It will. The question is whether you will help. Tonedeaf disaster. Obvious move would have been to focus on the graduates as the protagonists instead of framing them as accessories to AI's world takeover. And this is truly the issue. Everything the AI industry talks about is this race to this nebulous idea of AGI, which nobody outside of a very small group of people actually knows what that means. And even that very small group of people disagree on what it means. And so what Eric Schmidt is effectively saying is get on board and help build this thing that most of you believe is going to replace you. It is not a compelling story to be telling kids that are literally sitting there about to graduate. And now a little bit on the personal front. When I think about making videos, the videos that are almost guaranteed to do well, the ones that are generally negative on some topic, when Anthropic does something kind of crappy with their pricing or they don't treat their developers well or they're not transparent, calling them out, those videos tend to do really well. But there's also a lot of positive things in the AI industry happening that also deserve to be highlighted. And I feel like I have this tension in the business where if I want to get more views, I should probably dunk on Google or I should dunk on anthropic. And there are plenty of YouTubers who do that. But I am actually quite optimistic about the future. And I need to balance being optimistic while also being critical where it is warranted. And I know a lot of you in the comments have actually said things about it. Especially in the early days of my videos, you would always say, "Oh, of course Matt is hyping this up or Matt is excited about this. He never has anything negative to say." And I I took that to heart. Again, that was very valid feedback because I was so excited and I didn't want to dunk on builders. I am a builder at heart. I did come from building startups and building companies and putting my absolute heart and soul into something and putting it out there and just to have people dunk on it was such a crappy feeling. And so I didn't want to do that. But then again, I came to realize there is plenty of room to be critical of companies and decisions they make while also painting an overall optimistic picture of the future. That is what I believe is also best to convey to everybody else. coming back to Andre Carpathy trying to figure out why I was so affected by him joining Anthropic and I realized that's the reason is because he is co-signing effectively everything anthropic has been saying that there's going to be complete job loss that they are the only company good enough to create artificial intelligence in a safe way that opens source doesn't deserve to exist. These are not things I believe in the slightest. And so I saw Andre Karpathy as this shining light of education and optimism. But maybe it just wasn't that. Or just to paint a nice little optimistic angle on it, maybe him joining will inject a bit of optimism into Anthropic. Let's put a bow on this. I want to be optimistic. I enjoy being optimistic. I think there's plenty of room to be critical and to criticize companies when they make bad decisions. And I'm going to try to strike a balance. Not always going to get it right. Always open to your feedback, of course. Drop it in the comments below. I do read that. And I just hope Anthropic becomes net more positive, net more optimistic with Andre Karpathy on the team now. And by the way, there are plenty of examples of Anthropic being extremely pessimistic, including an essay that they just put out saying, "We have 2 years before China takes over." Or I reviewed that fully

items.title: Google CEO: Agents, Open Source, Race to AGI, Cybersecurity, Chips, China
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: I'm the CEO of Ford Future and I am super excited to share a conversation with the man who has been leading Google for the last 10 years. Please help me welcome Sundar Pachai there.
items.transcriptSummary: I'm the CEO of Ford Future and I am super excited to share a conversation with the man who has been leading Google for the last 10 years. Please help me welcome Sundar Pachai there.

transcript.text: Can y'all hear me? All right, welcome. This is the dialogue stage. My name is Matt Berman. I'm the CEO of Ford Future and I am super excited to share a conversation with the man who has been leading Google for the last 10 years. Please help me welcome Sundar Pachai there. Hi, first of all, uh, great to be here. Thanks for doing it, Matt. And I love your content and I appreciate all of you joining as well. >> You have a unique insight and probably very strong opinions about the future of the internet. And so that's where I want to start. It seems like the internet is being transformed right before our eyes. Agents are being built. They're infiltrating the internet. And I'm wondering, do you see the future as agents being the entry point to the internet for most people? You know, agents are going to be a fundamental part of how we work because having used them, you know, if you're living, you know, maybe today the people who are on the frontier of how agents work are developers, right? You know, and particularly with coding, you know, two years ago, most developers started using these tools. they they started giving them more and more auto completions and you were accepting them etc. But over the last few months, developers are actually doing agentic workflows, right? The the developers on the frontier and they are actually deploying agents, orchestrating agent. You saw the demo in anti-gravity for building an OS, you're effectively in an agentic workflow. So I think once you get the taste of using something like that and the superpower that comes with it I think so I think it's they're genuinely adding value in a way I think people will will use them. I think it's important we build it in a way that users feel a sense of control and agency and transparency when they use agents. I think that's important. But I think yes, I do expect agents to be a core part of how we use the web, but that doesn't mean it'll take away from people use the web for a lot of reasons, right? And you're entertaining yourself. You're trying to do something meaningful at times. And you know, it depends on your shopping, what you're shopping for. You know, if it's your weekly groceries versus you're trying to buy your loved one a gift, right? And so I think it'll allow humans to use the internet in ways that gives them joy and purpose and not always be forced to deal with I have to fill these 18 bomb fields to renew a DMV license. Right. So, you know, so that's how agents will separate it out. I think yeah, I mean I'm I'm particularly excited to have agents actually do real world tasks. You mentioned the DMV. That use case is incredible. Um, but I also think about putting so much trust into agents to really uh be the arbiter of our information diet and and I'm wondering how how do we make sure that that we are putting the trust in the right agents and the agents are deciding correctly what information that we should have. you know, you are doing a version of that if you use Gmail and there's a spam filter working on your behalf, filtering spam like in some ways you're like trusting an agent. It is an agent. You know, I I look at it as working on Whimo. You have to make people trust sitting on the backseat of a Whimo and let the car go. You know, that is an agent in some ways, but people are willing to trust. But that's because we've done the work over time to demonstrate to people both with data with how we have operated it that it's it's fundamentally safe and it's freeing you up to enjoy the ride. I think it's all depends on the value you deliver, right? I think that's why I think the building the agency and trust with users is a shared journey and we have to get that part right. Part of the reason in Gemini Spark, Gemini Spark is actually very powerful under the hood, but we are taking the deliberate careful step of making it work with your first party surfaces like Gmail, calendar, etc. before we expose third party with MCP and full computer use and browser use. You know, it can do all that, but we want to make sure users are in control and they feel comfortable. We are getting their feedback, improving the product as we give those capabilities. >> You know, Sundar, I if I'm crossing the street, you mentioned Whimo. I actually trust walking in front of a Whimo more so than I trust walking in front of a human driver. I I don't know if other people agree with that, but there there is it was almost an immediate trust that I had for Whimo. So, I'm I'm hoping agents are the same way. Um, I'm old enough to remember the early days of the internet, which was an absolute wild west, but you got to raw information very quickly. And I think, you know, part of something I'm concerned about is that we're increasing the the buffer between us and the raw internet. We saw it, you know, a little bit with the browser, a little bit with apps, and now more so with agents. How do we account for that? people in our experience with search and in YouTube, YouTube is a great example, right? People have this sense of connection with the creators they like and follow, right? And you know, so that's a big part of what they're looking for. So I think people are in different mindsets when there are times they want to discover content on the web like shopping is delightful for a lot of people in a lot of moments, right? And so they're not trying to fully outsource that, right? like you know that's why I try to distinguish between what feels like something which may be a chore at times and what feels like something which is delightful >> similarly I think you know uh it could be in news it could be people have their trusted sources at at least at Google through search and YouTube and through agents we think you know there will always be an incredible value from the ecosystem which users want to connect to and the agents should be in the job of doing that. >> Yeah. >> Um but you are right. There are times the agents are playing a role in the middle. Sometimes it's very good because you know it helps improve user satisfaction because users are getting to what they want in a better way. But but there's a layer of abstraction too and that's what you're talking about. Yeah, >> but I think it's always been true with technology a bit uh uh but I don't think ultimately you know it's also make at the same time it's also in the tools with which you can create content are also exploding so people will also be creating more content so I think there'll be a new balance which we will find but yeah it is an interesting moment of evolution. Yeah, I I like that. I think I think you're saying there's definitely going to be a strong place for agents to do that curation on our behalf, especially in the era of complete uh slop domination. >> Uh but also, you know, that feeling of exploration can still be there. >> Yes. >> Um Okay. Because it's a fundamental human need that doesn't go away. >> Yeah. Exactly. Um Okay. So speaking of the wild west and I I know you're probably feeling this pretty strongly on the uh at Google there have been increasing cyber security cyber attacks the models are getting better at cyber um obviously Google has been thinking about cyber security for decades are you seeing cyber attacks especially AI enhanced cyber attacks ramp up uh at Google >> look we've been seeing being very clear like we've deeply cared about cyber uh you know because we've been working on frontier technologies for a while like Google pioneered many of uh important security frontiers like zero trust and so on right like you know we we've worked hard to keep the company at the frontier and also we operate many products and platforms around the world that that touch billions of people I we've been pretty aggressive in deploying agent Agentic workflows. Our internal security teams use agentic workflows to help detect vulnerabilities and then how do you work to patch them? And we we have steadily seen over the last two years as the model capabilities have progressed you know we are able to detect more vulnerabilities and we've been working very hard to patch them. I think mythos was a point of inflection of capturing that moment in time. Um you know that they put out a model which was really you know well built for that particular task and it was frontier there right but what we are excited about it is part of the reason we are sharing maybe one of the uh undermentioned announcements today at iOS code mener. So codemener is a product which we use internally and which we're building to share externally. It not only helps you identify the vulnerabilities, generate patches, test and verify that they work and deploy them >> and it's running 24/7. >> That's right. And like you know and and real time we completed our recent acquisition of Viz, you know. So Viz is state-of-the-art in being able to do this realtime monitoring uh of of vulnerabilities etc. So I think combination of what we have with viz and code mender I think we are using it internally uh to stay at the frontier and I think it's an important moment for the industry. I have to say I'm heartened by the cross industry collaboration going on at this moment. uh I think one of the examples I would call out today be it synth ID or watermarking companies coming together companies coming together around cyber that is so important for this industry with this technology right so I'm encouraged by uh those trends as well >> so you mentioned mythos so I want to talk about that for a moment obviously anthropic decided not to release mythos publicly just a handful of companies we have open AAI releasing releasing uh GPT 5.5 cyber which which approach do you think is more appropriate which is right for Google is it uh you know is there is there some model that is just too good and and you're going to hold it back or is the more iterative deployment strategy that open AAI takes more aligned with what Google believes I think it depends on where you we it depends on the model capability. If it's if it is not fundamentally changing what's out there already in terms of the state-of-the-art, I think it's definitely okay to put it out. But in the security world, there's a well established practice. Google has done project zero for a long time, right? And like you know we have teams of people who find vulnerabilities then we notify the vendor give them 90 days to patch it before we acknowledge the vulnerability in the in the wide and so there are wellestablished practices in the security industry around how to do it. So I think it makes sense to me if you suddenly have something which dramatically changes the frontier. First of all, I think it's important to work closely with the government on that >> and and you approach it in a responsible way. So, so I I think that is consistent with how the security industry works and but I do think it's important to also def make sure enough people get access to it so they can patch their systems and so on and and they go hand in hand. So I think there's validity to that approach. So is there some threshold by which you would say past that point uh we can't release it or or is it more let's look at what the landscape of model uh capabilities are let's look at how cyber is right now and let's make the determination on a model basis that's what I would say like is the next one you're introducing does it dramatically change the frontier is it a 1 to 2% improvement over the current state-of-the-art or are you taking these 20% jumps. That's where the judgment comes in and I think that's how I would change my approach. So on the topic of model strategy, something near and dear to my heart is open source. Uh basically Google and Nvidia are the only companies with a real model uh open source model strategy nowadays. Um you know Google's model is smaller meant to run on edge devices. Why not release a large open-source frontier model? Look, we um first of all, Google, we've been big fans of open source. Google was built on a lot of open source systems. We have worked on many big things which are open source. I mean, I personally worked on Chromium and Android and so on, Kubernetes and I can name many projects which Google has contributed pretty strongly to the world in open source. In AI, we've been building Gemma models and we've been updating them year after year, right? And they're awesome and and uh you know and I think the recent release of Jimma 4 was a great release and you know so we are pushing it. I think all of us are trying to make you know make sure the frontier takes a lot of investment to get the frontier done right you've you've seen our capex dollars right and like you know so you're working you're putting a lot of R&D dollars to generate those incremental frontier models so I think and and you you're you're discovering new techniques as part of doing those models so we all have to be mindful of that but I think we are also committed to making sure there's a open source ecosystem which is able to develop and and and so we we take a balanced approach there uh approach there and I think we'll continue to take that balanced approach that's how I see it. Yeah. And and by the way, I do love the Gemma models. I run them locally at home. They are fantastic. So definitely thank you. Um you know, obviously a company of the size and the resources of Google, if you're making that decision of okay, large closed source, large open source, we can't do both. Um probably most startups in the US also will struggle with that decision. Like what what is your sense of the business model for open source in America? It is it is it viable right now? Look first of all we not only do open source part of the reason we invest so much in flashlight and flash models is so that we are giving a range of options right and and those models are workh horses too support but your question on open source it's not that we don't you know we are we've been moving the open source frontier too there's a lot of very very good open source models like particularly from China which startups are adopting too >> yeah we're going to talk about that >> so you know I think I think it depends right You go through moments in technology where the frontier moves so fast maybe sometimes the open source may not be able to fully keep up with it but then there are moments where open source will take leaps if the technology curve slows down or you know and you know takes a break right so uh it's tough to predict pred predict it fully in the future uh but I expect there to be a demand for a strong open-source ecosystem and I think We will definitely play a part in it and I hope others do do too. There there have been a number of open source ecosystems that have been really successful over many decades in technology. But I think just the upfront cost of of baking a model makes it extremely difficult especially if you're putting out the model and then all of a sudden your competitors are serving inference at a higher margin than you. Um but I I am hopeful. Uh, I I do love open source and we're going to talk about the workhorse models in a moment, but I want to talk about China and and they have been putting out incredible open-source models. Um, you know, if you can put yourself uh in the shoes of another uh enterprise CEO and you're looking at the landscape of which AI model to choose for your business and you're seeing, you know, deepseek at a fraction of the cost but still near the frontier. Uh why wouldn't America adopt Chinese open source AI? What's the argument to go with American AI? >> So you're saying why wouldn't uh uh you just use the best open source models available? Yeah. >> Look, I think you look at the end of the day, what are companies trying to do, right? They're trying to solve problems, right? These are and and they're trying to solve a problem with a solution. So the question is what are the solutions available? Remember they are designed let's say you're doing something in customer service you want predictability you want reliability >> like you want consistency and like you know so you want safety security so companies are optimizing for a lot of factors I think that gives a place for both open source models and there'll be providers who will take the open-source models and like build that ecosystem around it which makes a lot of sense there'll be closed source models and and you know it'll be open marketplace and people will have a lot of choice. I think I am more okay if it is open source with the right licenses it should matter less where it came from right like you know I think over time there are good ways to inspect open source not saying it's exactly true particularly with the how the AI models are developed but with open source comes a community which is responsible for it cares about it so if something is wrong is happening in that software it's not like it's going to go unnoticed So I think that creates a level of trust for people to adopt that technology. I think so I worry less about are we adopting open source models from China or more that are we doing enough in the US to make sure we are staying at the frontier. >> Yeah, that's how I think about it. >> Now I know like Google is is big on co-design full stack and and kind of just continuing on on where the open source model is coming from. I've heard that argument that is if it's open source it doesn't necessarily matter. we're going to fine-tune it or customize it for our needs. But ultimately, if we continue to build on top of, you know, China's open source, there's also an argument that they're going to optimize their models for their own chips and then all of a sudden we're kind of built on uh another country's technology. Is is that an incorrect argument? Look, I I think the fundamentals of AI, the way people should be building use cases on top is because the models are changing so fast anyway, you need to build it in a way in which you're able to evolve the models underneath, right? I think that has got to be the way, you know, you're working through this moment, right? And so I so I think you have to be dynamic enough that you have to be able to adapt because the model frontier the model ecosystems are changing pretty sharply and so that's the way I would think about it now and you know it's too early to predict if this is a real concern or not. >> Yeah. Um okay talking about model strategy one of my favorite things is watching the frontier labs and seeing how their model strategies play out. uh anthropic and open AI seem almost exclusively focused on the absolute frontier and Google has that but you guys also put a lot of emphasis on on what I'll call kind of the the workhorse class of models the flash class of models talk a little bit about why why is that such a big part of Google's strategy >> look I I mean in our mission statement we have this thing to to make technology universally accessible and useful >> we've always deeply cared for what is the most important technology in our lifetimes that it diffuses as broadly as possible and we get really excited at driving efficiency and making sure the best models can work in the fastest possible way cheaper because we need to do it for search right because we have to give it to billions of people >> we want to put it in Gemini and so we want to give it to developers so that they can do powerful things with And we've had a lot of success with the strategy and I think 3.5 flash particularly I made this point during the keynote but I've heard anecdotally from a lot of CIOS who are so concerned about how much their companies are blowing through budgets. >> Yeah. That you can feel it talking to them and I think the problem is going to get worse as we go through the year. Right. And I think that's where I think the flash model will really shine because particularly in a agentic workflow where you need these things to be repeatedly used and used a lot of times. I think it's so important to have a model which is very capable but is fast and efficient right and and even accounting for token effic token use flash is remarkably costefficient right and and and so I'm really excited we are finding it internally we are using it as a blend of pro and flash right internally and and I think most companies should learn to use it that way to be very clear we're super committed to being at the frontier on every category. I'm excited for Pro which we're working on. Uh but I think flash has a unique role to play in this constrained compute constraint time. Yeah. No, I I I agree completely especially most companies are are not, you know, solving math olympiad problems. They're not at the absolute cutting edge of science. They need real work done and that that is truly where the flash model shines. and not everybody is token maxing and trying to or unc unconcerned with the budget. So I I definitely appreciate that. Um I like if you think about the future of AI, is it just truly a race to self-improving AI? is is it like maybe just to play the the devil's advocate for a second the flash family of models are great now but ultimately whoever reaches self-improving AI first wins and then nothing else m do you think about it like that look I think first of all there's a responsibility that comes with this technology and you know I think we all need to be careful to avoid this race condition at all costs >> uh and and and and and and you know I think we owe it to humanity to make sure we deploy this technology responsibly. You are right in your question that there is this current moment where people feel like the curve is so steep and like you know >> uh you know where you are in the curve matters but just like in a few months ago when we launched 3.0 0 people are like oh we are so in the frontier no one will ever be able to catch up and you know I think at the frontier labs uh it's very dynamic the competition is fierce we all have our strengths and weaknesses we all also have different cadences of our pre-train pre-training release cycles so the peaks don't exactly match so all this creates that perception gap which shifts widely in like four to six weeks But I think a few few labs are really at the frontier and then there's a big gap. Um and I think I think there are scenarios in which things like recursive self-improvement come into play. But I think if they come into play with that no difference from the cyber moment, we all have to handle those moments far more responsibly than today. Right? And so that goes hand in hand. And so I I think the more AI becomes advanced, the more it's a societal conversation versus a single company conversation. >> Yeah. Yeah. Well said. So all of uh we know we're talking about a lot about models, but all of it is downstream from compute. Uh I I'm always both kind of uh impressed and in awe of Google's ability to serve, you know, you're serving your own models in inference on the API. You're also powering your suite of products with Gemini to literally billions of users. You're also allowing your competitors to use your inference. You're also selling TPUs. Um I've I've heard the reason for this is because I mean Thomas told me he said we planned really well which you know makes a lot of sense. You've been at this for 10 plus years. I think I've also heard that Google's revenue is literally constrained by compute. So I wanted to give you the opportunity what is the state of compute at Google right now? Look, I all of us are I think we have made a set of you know bold right decisions over the last few years to invest in compute and scale it up aggressively. But having said that I don't think any of us sit in the chair and say we wish we we had you know you look back and say I wish I had done a little bit more. So we're living in one of those moments in time and you know there are costs going up too. So like for a given budget you may be getting less compute than you had previously planned for your memory prices what have you you know so the costs are going up. I think we plan you know we are able to plan longterm for cloud separately from our you know own internal needs and we do long range plans and we plan for it. I think it's good and some of it is like when you're in Google cloud and you're supporting customers your your customers may look at something and say well I want access to that like they may look at the demo of 3.5 flash on anti-gravity and say how are you exactly running it at 800 tokens per second and like could we get access to that you know so you're also supporting customers through those journeys and hence you're meeting them in the in in what they're asking for and but it is not an easy balancing act and You know, we are we are constantly thinking as far ahead as possible and we are making tradeoffs uh like every other company right now. And >> so do you have maybe an obvious question more demand than you have compute to serve it? >> Absolutely. >> Like what is the scale of that? >> And and and hence the emphasis on something like 3.5 flash even more >> right and you know could we have done an even better omni model? Yes. But like you know how can we do a omni model which we can give to as many people as possible. So there's constantly you're making trade-offs of trade-offs of like that including on you know do you build a very large model which you know like an ultraized model that will increase the capability frontier but then who all can you give it to? So you're constantly all of us are making these trade-offs and sometimes you make a trade-off it looks like well maybe you done that trade-off a bit differently. Yeah. So we only have a few seconds left. One last question for you. What is the main bottleneck for compute for Google right now? Is it land? Is it political will? What is it? I think I think the way it works is by definition bottlenecks work this way. If you think something is a bottleneck and you solve it, something else becomes a bottleneck. Right? Like you know that's the whole like definition of bottlenecks. I think at various times I think you're having a few areas your ability to physically cons permit and construct data centers the power they need in and then very quickly you get into the core components for these systems they are all the bottleneck. >> Yeah. and and it's a it's it's like you have you need all of that to work together to get a square set of a chip you need right and like you know and so uh I feel like there are a few parallel bottlenecks going through and it almost doesn't matter there are like a few of them which are bottlenecks and at various times you may you may conclude it is memory but then if everyone concludes it's memory tomorrow you turn around and say okay no no no it's it's actually this and so you know but but I think there are systemic bottlenecks across all layers of the stack now >> yeah whatever the bottleneck is in the moment that's the most compute you can have in that moment okay everybody please help me thank Darpachai thank you so much thank you thank you thank you guys awesome

items.title: So Anthropic is just winning now
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: We all knew Anthropic was absolutely ripping, but for the first time they actually took over enterprise usage from the king OpenAI. Rams AI index report, which they just put out, shows for the first time 34.4% of businesses are using Anthropic versus 32.3 using OpenAI.
items.transcriptSummary: We all knew Anthropic was absolutely ripping, but for the first time they actually took over enterprise usage from the king OpenAI. Rams AI index report, which they just put out, shows for the first time 34.4% of businesses are using Anthropic versus 32.3 using OpenAI.

transcript.text: We all knew Anthropic was absolutely ripping, but for the first time they actually took over enterprise usage from the king OpenAI. Rams AI index report, which they just put out, shows for the first time 34.4% of businesses are using Anthropic versus 32.3 using OpenAI. Look at this growth here. In the tan color, Anthropic is just going absolutely vertical for basically the last year. It looks like OpenAI has been essentially flat, but as we can also see in black, the overall adoption of artificial intelligence in enterprise has been going up and up, but still kind of surprising that only 50% of all businesses are using artificial intelligence. By the way, all of this is reflected in Anthropic's revenue, which has far surpassed OpenAI's revenue. In fact, just about a week ago, Dario, the CEO of Anthropic, said that their growth rate was 80x, unheard of in the history of business. They are well on their way to becoming the biggest company in the entire world.

items.title: $400,000 from Claude Code

items.summary: It already has millions of views just after a few hours. Somebody loses their crypto password, their Bitcoin password, and suddenly they are locked out.
items.transcriptSummary: It already has millions of views just after a few hours. Somebody loses their crypto password, their Bitcoin password, and suddenly they are locked out.

transcript.text: All right, this story is crazy. It already has millions of views just after a few hours. We've all heard this story before. Somebody loses their crypto password, their Bitcoin password, and suddenly they are locked out. They can no longer access their Bitcoin. In fact, one guy spent years digging through the dump trying to find his USB wallet. And there are all kinds of these stories just like this guy. He lost the key to his crypto wallet nine years ago. He spent $250 per Bitcoin that is now worth about $80,000 each. But then, Claude code saved him. He literally gave Claude code access to his entire college computer. And within minutes, Claude code found a way to recover the key. He said he tried 7 trillion passwords. And the best part is, this is the password.

transcript.text: This is the most concrete essay that I have seen a major AI lab put out. Anthropic is panicking. They present only two scenarios for the future of AI. And in this video, I am going to review this essay in full and show you why what they're saying is so important. And if you like seeing me cover new articles like this, give this video a like, subscribe to the channel. It really does help the channel. and thank you in advance. And by the way, this entire video is brought to you by here.now. More on them in a minute. This article starts with such a strong statement. 2028, two scenarios for global AI leadership, and it's going to become clear why they specifically called out the 2028 date as I go through this paper. It's essential that the US and its allies stay ahead of authoritarian governments like the Chinese Communist Party or CCP. They minced no words. They are specifically saying it is of utmost importance that the United States and its allies stay ahead of China in the race for artificial intelligence. So before we get into this, I know a lot of you watch from throughout the entire world and I just want to acknowledge my perspective comes from being born in America. So with that, let's get into the paper. AI will soon become powerful enough to be used to repress citizens at unprecedented scale and even to alter the balance of power among nations. And since AI is advancing more quickly by the day, we have only a limited period of time to set the conditions of the competition and determine whether and how those threats materialize. It's with this in mind that we outline what's required to ensure America stays ahead. So once again, not mincing any words, of course, this American enterprise wants America to win. Okay. So as I go through this, I believe Anthropic actually nailed a lot of their arguments. In fact, their identification of risk to America's dominance in artificial intelligence was spot-on in almost every way. The part where I really diverge from Anthropic's point is the solutions to the problem. And so I'm going to tell you my thoughts as we go through this. The most important ingredient for developing AI is access to the computer chips on which the models are trained. So they're saying more so than the data, more so than the researchers, more so than anything else, the actual chips, the part that they don't really control themselves is the most important part. So think Nvidia, Google with their TPUs, AWS with Trrenium and Anthropic and OpenAI. The two leading AI model companies have no silicon themselves. They only buy and rent from others. Since the most capable chips are developed by American companies, the US government currently limits China supply by enforcing tight export controls on them. And this is already where there is a huge divergence in opinion between US companies on whether export controls are good or bad, whether they're working, whether there are just too many loopholes and they're not really working at all. And it's just so interesting to see such a strong stance and such an opposite stance from Nvidia in particular. Recent history suggests these controls have been incredibly successful. Okay, we're going to get into that. In fact, AI labs in China have only built models close in intelligence to Americas because of their talent, their knack for exploiting loopholes around these export controls and their large-scale distillation attacks that illicitly extract the innovations of American companies. So, first, because of China's talent, there's a clip in which Jensen says >> 50% of the world's AI researchers are Chinese. So, we already know China and the Chinese people more broadly are incredibly good at AI research. Then on top of that, their knack for exploiting loopholes around these export controls. We've heard lots of both rumors as well as proven stories about smuggling chips from one country to another, using proxies to get inference and compute on chips that China is not supposed to have access to. We've heard it all already. So, they are definitely doing this. And their large-scale distillation attacks that illicitly extract the innovations of American companies. Now, this one's very interesting. Anthropic is linking to their own report about Chinese distillation attacks, but what's interesting is I don't know if it's actually considered large scale. In fact, many people countered a lot of these findings with nope, this is actually relatively small on the scale of distillation attacks. In fact, Deepseek was on the very small side. And there could be a justification of we were just running competitive benchmarks against competing models. And if you're not familiar with what distillation is, it means taking a big model, getting it to answer questions, and that data gets used to feed into a smaller model to make that smaller model really good, but at a fraction of the cost and size. And by the way, if you're watching this video, I know that you are deep in artificial intelligence. And if you want to try out one of the coolest agentic products I have ever seen, check out the sponsor of today's video. Here. Now is one of those products where once you see it, it just becomes so obvious what the future will look like. Here. Now is web hosting for agents. If you ever need your agent to publish anything, which is all the time for me, whether I'm sharing documents with my team or I need to send something to another agent, I always need to publish. I literally just tell my agents, "Publish this to here.now. It's free and you don't even need an account to use it." And the best part, it is agentnative. And that is what makes it so special. And it works with any agent. Claude Code, OpenClaw, Cursor, Codeex, any of them. Go to here. Now, copy the setup instructions and install it. And you're done. That's it. You now have instant publishing for your agent. And they just added private storage as well. So if you don't want to publish something publicly, simply tell your agent, go store it and it will. And that's it. You can access it from anywhere. It is private to you and your agent. And the best part is there's no interface. It is all done through whatever agent you're using. I am a massive fan of here. Now I use it with all of my agents. I highly recommend checking it out. Thanks to here. Now for sponsoring this video. Now back to the video. So then they go on to describe two scenarios for what 2028 will look like. So they don't explicitly say why they keep talking about 2028, why that year, but I have a very good idea. One, it's probably just the right amount of time for the US to either sustain and solidify its lead or China officially catches up. But what I actually think is more likely is that we are going to have self-improving artificial intelligence by 2028. And whoever hits self-improving AI first essentially just wins. And this is different from what anthropic says in this paper. I'll get more into that in a moment. Okay. So two scenarios. In the first scenario, America has successfully defended its compute advantage. Policymakers have acted to tighten export controls further, disrupt China's distillation attacks, and further accelerate democracy's adoption of AI. These are all very aspirational and good things for us in this world. Democracies set the rules and norms around AI. Now, let me tell you when it's the easiest to negotiate with somebody, when you have leverage, okay, it's very difficult, almost impossible to negotiate with somebody when they have all the power. And that's essentially what Anthropic just said. They said, "Hey, we'll be able to negotiate with China on AI safety if we have the most powerful AI models." Sure. And we will also be able to dictate what those policies are because we do have the most powerful models. But there is a second scenario. In this second scenario, America has chosen not to act. Now, this is important how they're saying this because this is basically framing their solution to the problems as the only solution. And of course, I don't like that and I disagree with their solutions. But again, we'll get back to that in a little bit. Policymakers have not tightened loopholes on the CCP's access to compute and AI firms in China have quickly taken advantage, catching up to the frontier and even overtaking America. In this world, AI norms and rules are shaped by authoritarian regimes. And the best AI models enable automated repression at scale. It will be no solace that this authoritarian triumph has happened on the back of American compute. So basically what they're saying is that since America has basically invented all of these technologies around AI and then we're shipping China all of our hardware, all of our chips and then they're able to catch up and we no longer come into the negotiation from a position of power and leverage, we're probably going to be kicking ourselves. America and its allies approach AI competition from a position of great strength. The tools for AI dominance have been built by an exceptionally innovative ecosystem of companies and democratic nations. We know that the open- source models coming out of China are extremely capable. They're not frontier, but they're probably about 6 to n months behind what our public frontier is. We focus on the CCP as it is the regime that is most able to use frontier AI to cement authoritarianism. We do not seek to undermine the interests or ingenuity of the Chinese people. That is key. To make it clear, when I talk about America versus China, I am making a clear distinction between the Chinese government and the Chinese citizens. They are very distinct. Already the CCP is using AI to censor speech, repress dissident, hack governments and corporations across the world, and strengthen the People's Liberation Army, the PLA. And yes, they've not only been doing this recently, they've been doing it for many, many years. The amount of mass surveillance going on in China is truly stunning. And in fact, a lot of their early work in artificial intelligence, the reason why they weren't at the frontier with us while we were discovering it was because they spent so much time and research investment into technology and AI for mass surveillance, facial recognition, and the like. AI labs in China have worldclass talent. This is true. When you read some of these papers, especially from the Deepseek team, some of the innovation, some of the algorithmic unlocks that they've been able to achieve under such heavy constraint is beyond impressive. So, they have the talent. There is no argument against that. And I know I can hear the video comments already. The United States does surveillance. And yes, I am not naive to think that the US government is perfect and doesn't do some of these things. But overall, I believe in representative democracies as a way to curb some of those bad habits, whereas the authoritarian regime in China does not. Continuing, it is compute constraints that limit their ability to keep up. But this is temporary. Okay, this is temporary. The compute constraints only mean that they will scramble to develop their own chip ecosystem which they are already doing and is a big part of what I disagree with based on this anthropic essay. Labs in China have remained close by exploiting loopholes in US export control policies and by carrying out large-scale distillation attacks that harvest the innovations of US models in order to mimic their capabilities. Now, I am not saying that the Chinese model companies are not doing distillation attacks, but I'm not 100% convinced that the scale of these attacks are enough to keep China right on our tail on model innovations. I really do think a lot of the reason why they're just right behind us is because of their innovations on the algorithm side and just their raw talent, their raw human talent. So the paper goes on to explain how AI is accelerating, the amount of compute we need is accelerating, and thus right now is when we need to make policy decisions to try to solidify that lead for the foreseeable future. To date, by allowing export control, evasions, and distillation attacks. Now, who is allowing it? I don't think anybody is explicitly allowing it. I know that Anthropic is actively trying to prevent distillation attacks and so is every other major AI lab and we do have laws in place. We have export controls in place. And so if China is getting the chips anyways, it's not because we wanted them to. And just to clarify this point, I don't think the United States is somehow looking the other way and allowing China to get the chips that we do have export controls on or that somehow we're doing this to ourselves. I just don't believe that. And that is the sense that I'm getting anthropic believes by reading this paper. Many in Congress and the Trump administration have championed export controls, curbing distillation attacks, and exporting American AI. In advancing these policies, we are hopeful that democracies can secure a commanding lead by 2028 and avoid a destabilizing neck-and-neck race with the CCP 2 years from now. Now, here's the important part. The political systems in which the most advanced AI is created will shape the rules and norms for how the technology is developed and deployed. This is incredibly important. If you are the leader in AI technology, you get to dictate how it's deployed, how it's used, and really, you have the ultimate leverage. And that's from a geopolitical point of view. AI is the most important technology we have ever created. And whoever is in control of it is basically the decision maker on the world stage. Now, here's a few interesting bits from this essay. They specifically highlight how AI under an authoritarian regime can be more harmful than anything we've seen previously in history. Historically, the reach of authoritarian rule has been limited by its dependence on human enforcers to carry out surveillance and repression. But powerful AI systems may remove that dependency, enabling automated repression on a far greater scale. For that reason, the prospect of the CCP leading in AI is among the greatest threats to a successful transition. The technology of artificial intelligence is very much a double-edged sword. On the one hand, it allows every human to be orders of magnitude more productive and our efficiency in different technologies to be optimized beyond our wildest imaginations. But at the same time, that exact leverage can be applied to mass surveillance and enforcement. So that's what they're pointing out. And I could not agree more. items.title: Robot sets the sorting record
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: This is a humanoid robot running for 8 hours straight on a live stream sorting through packages. The most impressive part is that it's running at human speed making sure that the label just like that is face down.

It is just fun to see the number of packages go up and occasionally we'll see little awkward movements from the robot, but overall it's doing a fantastic job. And over an 8-hour period that's about 12,000 packages in total. I can tell you for sure that is more than what I would do. I would need to take a pee break.

items.title: Everyone's getting hacked
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: I've been following artificial intelligence for years, and for the first time I'm actually starting to get worried. It has gotten both easier and more profitable to commit cyber crimes, specifically cyber hacks.

items.title: Claude, The Pope, and AGI
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.
items.transcriptSummary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.

transcript.text: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. So let's talk about it. Spying on US citizens and autonomous weapons powered by AI were the crux of the issue. Anthropic refused to budge and Google and OpenAI became the defense contractors and Anthropic was designated a supply chain risk. But they've been scoring a lot of reputational points recently, both against their push back on the Department of War and recently getting Andre Karpathy to join the team who is very well respected in the industry by pretty much everybody both the academics and the researchers and the engineers and the AI enthusiasts. By the way, really fast, I've been saying this on this channel probably 100 plus times now since we started and that is AI will intersect with every part of everyone's lives. So he's one of the main people at Anthropic and he's one of the most important figures in AI interpretability. So this isn't a sales guy. This isn't an executive. This guy is a researcher. So the fact that more and more people are taking this seriously and talking about this is great. Google DeepMind recently hired a chief AGI economist or something like that. basically someone that's going to start looking into how to navigate that transition. The fact that the Vatican is talking about it is good, too. I don't know what their plans are, but the fact that more people are saying it and more people are hearing this message and we'll need to start kind of wrestling with it and trying to at least understand it, I think overall that's a good thing. The second big message that they're talking about is that these AI labs, well, they're not to just be trusted by default, so to speak. As the Pope said, AI is not neutral. The people that are building it, designing it, they're kind of infusing their own ideas into it. It's important that other people participate in the shaping of AI. And the third is kind of talking about what AI is, how it's a black box that we don't fully understand. And we need to be able to detect dangerous patterns as this thing gets better and better and smarter before it can cause realworld harm. And let's start there because I already see the most confusion out there about this and the thing that Christopher Ola was saying in one of the messages. He talked about the AI models having something that appears to us to be something like emotions or introspection or other things that are similar to what humans possess. And this is where a lot of the conversation breaks down again because words can be a little bit vague. They can have a very different meaning to different people. We covered a lot of research on this channel coming out about anthropic about how these models behave. We've read their paper about introspection about Claude and the other AI models have something that can be called introspection. And the third big thing that I think they're talking about is this idea that AI labs right now, as they say, hold too much power. Interestingly, the Pope's argument is not anti-technology. It's more pro-humanity or anti-dehumanization. The people that are building it, financing it, promoting it, like all those people have an impact and an effect on how this AI acts, behaves, what ideals it supports. items.title: OpenAI just SOLVED MATH....
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So 7 months ago, OpenAI claimed that one of their models solved a problem that no human or AI has ever solved before. This ended up being a great embarrassment for OpenAI.
items.transcriptSummary: So 7 months ago, OpenAI claimed that one of their models solved a problem that no human or AI has ever solved before. This ended up being a great embarrassment for OpenAI.

transcript.text: So 7 months ago, OpenAI claimed that one of their models solved a problem that no human or AI has ever solved before. Now this was incorrect. This ended up being a great embarrassment for OpenAI. Deis Hassabus calls it embarrassing. John Lun jumps in as well and says hoisted by their own GPTard. But seven short months later, OpenAI fires back. It says, "An openi model has disproved a central conjecture in discrete geometry." The date that they chose to publish this was the day that the Google IO is taking place. Obviously, there's a very well-known mathematician. This is his name. It's pronounced Erdish. And they're calling this the Erdish breakthrough. So, here's kind of the big point about this breakthrough. A lot of the critics that were criticizing OpenAI back then, a lot of the mathematicians, they're 100% on board with this. So, they're saying that was fake. This isn't. They checked the proof. They signed off on it. They confirmed that it is a novel discovery. An AI model all on its own found some new math that humans haven't been able to for decades and decades. So the big headline here is this most likely and very well is the first time that an AI model did the following. It produced a genuinely publishable result on a prominent open problem that's at the center of field of math. But out of this whole thing, there's one sentence that really stands out to me. It's from a Harvard mathematician who verified the whole thing. This mathematician said something that should maybe bother you because she wasn't criticizing the AI. She wasn't saying that the AI discovered alien math. She was pointing out something that this AI revealed about us. Okay, but let's rewind and let's break it all apart to find out exactly what's happening. First and foremost, the AI model in question is from OpenAI. It's an unreleased model. It's a general reasoning model. So just like whatever you're using in Chad GPT, it's a large language model made for general reasoning. Again, it's unreleased. It's an internal math model at OpenAI. And this model went up against one of the most recognizable names in mathematics, Paul Erish. And Erdish was famous for posing these very simple sounding problems that when you start to dig into it were actually brutally difficult. and he proposed this problem that we're talking about today called the planer unit distance in 1946. And the problem is very simple. Imagine you have nine dots. So nine of these little dots here. Your goal is to place as many of them so that they are exactly 1 in apart or one unit apart, whatever. So for example, if I take these nine dots, I put them in a straight line, we get eight connections that are exactly 1 in apart, right? So far pretty easy. or we can put them in a 3x3 grid and we're able to get 12 1-in connections. So, it look kind of like that, right? So, arranging them like this gives you more 1-in connections between those dots. So, this one wins. So, the question is, what happens if you have a hundred dots or a thousand or a billion or a trillion? As the numbers of dots and rows as they grow, how many connections can we have? how quickly the number of connections can grow. So you can even think of it as houses that have to be one block apart from each other in a city and you're just keep expanding and expanding a city. Is this the best sort of layout to get as many of those as possible? Erdish and a lot of other mathematicians that worked in this believed that this sort of grid, right? You can think of it as a city grid, right? So just just like you see here like this was the most effective and efficient layout. Now a lot of people discovered some little clever tricks how to set up these grids to actually pack in more dots and more connections. But Erish basically thought that these clever grids were the best possible design that this was unbeatable. Then the open AI model comes in and they go, "Oh, no, no, no. items.title: Google entered the "AGENTIC ERA"
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: Google just unleashed the era of agents. It teamed up with a chunker of a fund to build [music] out its Neo Cloud.
items.transcriptSummary: Google just unleashed the era of agents. It teamed up with a chunker of a fund to build [music] out its Neo Cloud.

transcript.text: Google I/O in 5 seconds flat. All life forms evolve into crabs. All tech evolves into agents. Google just unleashed the era of agents. It teamed up with a chunker of a fund to build [music] out its Neo Cloud. Also, search is cooked. Let's quickly go over everything you might have missed at the Google I/O. So, first and foremost, what's happening with the AI models? Did they drop the brand new giant model to amaze everybody? Well, no, not yet. They did announce the Gemini 3.5 series of models and they're releasing Gemini 3.5 Flash. That's going to be the new default model for the Gemini app and the AI mode in search. It has faster output, better coding, better agentic task handling, and a better multimodal performance. It has richer interactive UI generation, improved safety guardrails, etc. That's going to be kind of the daily driver for a lot of the Google stuff that's happening, but the big super smart model that we've been hearing some rumors about, the Gemini 3.5 Pro, that's still not out. They're saying it's still being tested and they're hoping to release it next month or coming next month were their exact words. They've also announced that the Gemini Omni and the Omni Flash. That's a new creation focused model family. It takes text, images, audio, and video as input. And as you might imagine, there's already a lot of people trying to oppose it, trying to slow it down, or prevent it from happening in in the way that Elon wants it to happen. Specifically, a lot of this hinges on this one provision that says that the CEO can only be removed by vote of class B shareholders. There's different groups of shares you can own, and some with voting privileges, some without. Basically, in a nutshell, what this means is that Elon can't get fired unless Elon agrees. Basically, he controls enough of the voting shares to where you can't remove him unless he agrees or he sells off some of his shares, which he already said he's not going to. Large pension funds in the US rebel against this move from coast to coast. Well, not quite coast to coast, it's mainly California and New York really, but um so both coasts both coasts are are pissed and they're opposing this strongly. New York State Comptroller, New York City Comptroller, CEO of CalPERS, a large California pension fund, they collectively represent three of the top four largest US public pension funds. Interestingly, their total amounts of assets under management is over one trillion. So, SpaceX seems like it will be bigger than the three of them, but still they hold a lot of sway. Now, if you haven't been following this whole saga of Elon versus it's not even the legal system at large. It's a certain specific segments of the legal system. Basically, back in the days for Tesla, Elon was supposed to receive the largest CEO compensation package in US corporate history, 56 billion or thereabouts. And basically, the deal was that Musk would receive zero payments, zero compensation, zero guaranteed pay. It was zero or nothing. items.title: AI just BROKE the ENTIRE INDUSTRY...

items.summary: And it's going to flip the entire finance industry on its head. Now, I know that sounds like a bold claim, but here's the thing.

And as we talked about maybe a few weeks ago, Anthropic is also not sitting still. They're going after Wall Street from the enterprise side. They launched 10 finance-specific AI agents for for banks, for insurers. So, I'm sure a lot of people know who Reid Hoffman is, right? So, he is the creator of LinkedIn. He's on the Microsoft board. He's a a big presence in this tech space. He published this morning an article on X talking about what to make of Anthropic's push into finance. So, he's saying Anthropic made three big financial moves. Number one, it was a $1.5 billion Hellman & Friedman and Goldman Sachs to put Claude into major private equity portfolio companies. So, we've talked about that before. So, Anthropic is targeting targeting Wall Street and trying to get embedded in there. At the same time, they have a partnership with FIS, which runs payment infrastructure for about 12% of the global economy. And there they're going to be building AI agents for financial crime investigation starting with money laundering. As Reed Hoffman put it, Anthropic is making a bet that AI is moving to become the plumbing of the financial system with Claude in the lead, which to me personally makes a lot of sense to have AI as the plumbing or the infrastructure for a lot of the stuff that's happening in the financial sector in finance as a whole, it does make sense. There are certain areas right now where it would make a massive difference where it's ready to go. Personal finance, help with things like that. I think it's ready and it's already would be an improvement if people really got to experience how good it is at that. Now, there's tons of stuff where obviously you got to be careful like would you let some AI agent, you know, file your taxes? Maybe that's a different story. You really want to make sure it doesn't make any errors. According to Reed Hoffman, so he's saying at the FI S partnership, right? So that's where these AI agents are going after money laundering, after various financial crimes. The AI agents were correct, they were right in about 64% of of the cases. The big question here is what is the human error rate, right? It's not 100%, we know that for a fact. As Reed Hoffman saying, human only AML compliance lives somewhere in the mid-70s on accuracy and costs roughly an order of magnitude more per decision. So the question isn't how do we get AI agents to be right 100% of the time because if it goes from 65% right to, let's say, 75%, it's on par with humans and much, much cheaper.
