items.title: Codex Superapp V2: OpenAI Just UNLOCKED Your Mac! Do you trust it?
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codeex use your Mac while it's locked. Deepseek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round.
items.transcriptSummary: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codeex use your Mac while it's locked. Deepseek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round.

transcript.text: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codeex use your Mac while it's locked. Deepseek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round. Enthropic just dropped their first real update from Project Glasswig. Enthropic also quietly made a partnership that's going to change a lot of things. So, let's get into it. OpenAI has a weekly release cadence for Codeex now and they're calling this Codeex Thursday. So, four updates went out this week and the one I want to focus on is locked computer use. So, here's what it does. Codex can now operate on your Mac while your Mac is locked. You send a task from your phone. The laptop handles it and it stays locked the whole time. Some background on how we got here. Codex already had a computer use feature on Mac OS. You would install a plugin, give it screen recording and accessibility permissions, and Codex can interact with your apps. It can click on Windows, type, navigate menus, use the clipboard, same actions you would do manually. The limitation was that your Mac had to be unlocked and awake, which made remote use kind of pointless. You would basically need to leave your laptop open and logged in for the agent to do anything. This update, however, removes that limitation. There's a new toggle in Codex settings called lock computer use. And when you enable it, Codex installs an Apple authorization plugin that participates in the Mac OS unlock flow. After that, you can send tasks from the Codex mobile app and Codex can run them on your lock Mac. The obvious concern is, is this a back door into your laptop? OpenAI addresses this directly in the documents. They describe locked use as intentionally narrow. It's not a general purpose remote unlock path and other apps or local processes can't use it to unlock your computer. Only codecs can and only during what they call active trusted computer use turns. What that means in practice, when a codeex task starts, the system checks whether the unlock attempt is tied to a real in progress computer use turn. If it is, the Mac unlocks. If it isn't, the unlock is denied and you have to log in manually. While the task is running, Codex covers every display. Anyone walking past your desk sees the lock screen overlay, which says Codex is using your Mac. Press any key to take over. They don't see the apps Codex is actually interacting with. The other safeguard is input detection. If Codex sees any keyboard or mouse activity locally, it relocks the Mac immediately and pauses automatic unlock until you log in by hand. Also, touching your computer ends the session. There are limits on what Codeex can actually do in this mode. Agent skills, hooks, sub agents, extensions. The extensions get renamed to anti-gravity plugins, but they work the same way. Code execution, file management, web browsing, the reasoning loop. You pull all of that into your own product, define custom agent behaviors, and host them wherever you want. In practice, you would use this to drop an agent into your own SaaS app, build internal tools for your engineering team, or wire agents into your CI/CD pipeline. It's optimized for Gemini, but you can swap models. Obviously, you would use this if you're building AI agents into your own products. You would skip it if you just want to use them. So, now the lineup should make sense once you see it laid out. One agent brain underneath, four surfaces on top. IDE if you want to write the code yourself, 2.0 app if you want to manage agents instead, CLI for the terminal, and SDK for building your own agents. The product strategy is solid, the launch was the problem. Three things went wrong. Number one, the default download became the new app instead of the IDE. Second, the auto update silently swapped your editor with the agent manager. And third, there was almost no in-app messaging telling you what happened or where your IDE went. items.title: OpenAI's Secret Model Just Did Something Nobody Thought Was Possible!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: OpenAI just solved a problem that seemed impossible for over 80 years and dropping hard the biggest name in AI and cursor just shipped a model that matches GPT-5.5 and Claude 4.7 at a tenth of the cost. An internal OpenAI model just disproved a math conjecture that Paul Erdos posted in 1946 and that mathematicians have been trying to settle for 80 years. Cursor says 2.5 is better at long-running agentic tasks, follows complex instructions more reliably, and handles sustained work better.
items.transcriptSummary: OpenAI just solved a problem that seemed impossible for over 80 years and dropping hard the biggest name in AI and cursor just shipped a model that matches GPT-5.5 and Claude 4.7 at a tenth of the cost. transcript.text: OpenAI just solved a problem that seemed impossible for over 80 years and dropping hard the biggest name in AI and cursor just shipped a model that matches GPT-5.5 and Claude 4.7 at a tenth of the cost. So, let's get into it. An internal OpenAI model just disproved a math conjecture that Paul Erdos posted in 1946 and that mathematicians have been trying to settle for 80 years. The model received the problem and produced an original proof on its own and once you read through the chain of thought, which is about 125 pages long, the whole conversation about what AI can do quietly shifts. The problem is called the planar unit distance problem. Take n points on a flat plane, count how many pairs are exactly one unit apart, figure out the maximum. For almost 80 years, every serious mathematician working on this assumed that square grid arrangements were essentially optimal. That belief is now dead. The OpenAI model produced a new family of construction that beats the grid and the construction uses ideas from algebraic number theory, which is a totally different branch of math that the one this problem normally lives in. The proof has been verified. External mathematicians, including some of the top names in the field, checked it and wrote a companion paper explaining the argument and signed off on it. Greg Brockman is saying that this is the first time AI has autonomously solved a prominent open problem central to a field of math. Noam Brown from OpenAI described the model as a general-purpose LLM, which means this model was not fine-tuned for math or not trained on this problem, just a general reasoning model. Now, here's why this is really important. OpenAI actually released the chain of thought, which is 125 pages of the model's actual reasoning while it worked on this problem. And it's not what you think. It's not the model trying things and getting lucky. It's the model exploring lattice constructions and killing that approach, trying hypercube constructions, killing those, trying other approaches and working through roots of unity, ruling out CM fields based on unit rank arguments, and so on and so forth. It is reading like a serious mathematician thinking out loud, including the dead ends, the self-corrections, and the moments where it catches its own mistakes. This is the part that changes the conversation because for the last 2 years, the loudest critiques of these models has been, "Sure, they're impressive, but they're just remixing patterns from trading data. They can't do real discovery. They can't extend that frontier." This is real discovery and is reproducible. OpenAI published the test time compute curve. The more inference compute you give the model on this problem, the higher the pass rate climbs. So, this isn't a one-off lucky run. There's a scaling relationship between compute and how often the model can produce a valid proof. If you step back and look at what just happened, an AI system, on its own, took an 80-year-old open problem in one of the most established fields of mathematics and pushed the frontier. Mathematicians did not give it the strategy. They didn't give it hints. They gave it the problem statement and it did the rest. The companion paper from the external mathematicians has a line in it that I think is worth quoting. They say the frontiers of knowledge are very spiky, and the coming months and years will see similar successes in many other areas of mathematics where long-standing open problems are resolved by AI revealing unexpected connections. That's the part to internalize. This is not about which model is better than which model. If a general-purpose internal OpenAI model can do this in math today, then the same kind of system can do it in physics, in biology, in chemistry, in materials, in places where the open problems have actual economic and scientific weight behind them. We've been arguing for 2 years about whether these systems can do real research. That argument is over. They can. The only remaining questions are how fast, in how many fields, and what happens next. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. While OpenAI was busy dropping the math proof of the year, Anthropic was quietly making a hire that might matter for the long run. Andrej Karpathy just joined Anthropic. If you've been around AI for any amount of time, you know the name. Karpathy is one of the co-founders of OpenAI. He was the director of AI at Tesla. He runs the Eureka Labs. His YouTube tutorials on building neural nets from scratch are basically the textbook for an entire generation of AI engineers. He's one of the few people who can actually bridge LLM theory and the practical reality of training these models at scale. He posted a file line update on X yesterday. He's joining Anthropic. He's working on pre-training under Nick Joseph, and he's specifically helping start a new team focus on using Claude itself to accelerate pre-training research. Anthropic is hiring one of the best AI researchers alive to figure out how to use your own model to make the next model better. Karpathy is not an isolated case. He's the latest in a pattern that's been quietly building for a year. The CTO of Workday left to become a member of technical staff at Anthropic. Same with the CTO of u.com, the CTO of Instagram, the CTO of Box, the CTO of super.com, the CTO of Adept. These are people running engineering at billion-dollar companies, and they're not joining Anthropic to run a division. They're taking individual contributor research roles. They're going back to writing code and training models. When senior people leave money, equity, and titles to do hands-on and research somewhere else, that's the strongest signal you can get in this industry. It's a bet with your career on the line, and right now the bet keeps landing on Anthropic for some reason. And if you tie in the OpenAI story, OpenAI just showed that one of their internal models can disprove an 80-year-old math conjecture. That's a public win for them. Anthropic doesn't have a comparable splashy announcement this week, at least at the moment, but what they do have is that the talent is flying towards Anthropic. The AI race is usually framed around compute and funding. Anthropic just hit $1 trillion valuation. They struck a compute deal with SpaceX. They're not losing the funding war or the chip war, but the key variable that actually decides which lab ships the model that changes everything next year is which lab has the people who can build it. And the people who can build it are the small pool of researchers who've been around long enough to know what works. That pool is choosing Anthropic right now. Karpathy is the loudest name on that list, and he's not the only one. And when you stack the OpenAI math breakthrough next to a year of hires like this, the picture you get isn't one lab winning, it's two labs running flat out. And the next 12 months are going to be ridiculous, and I'm super excited for them. Cursor just shipped Composer 2.5. It's an upgrade to their in-house coding model, and the interesting part isn't the benchmarks, it's what they actually trained for. Quick context. Composer is Cursor's own coding model, the one that powers their actual AI agent inside the editor. Composer 2 came out in March 2026, and Composer 2.5 is the follow-up. It's built on the same base as Composer 2, which is Moonshot's Kimiko 2.5 open-source checkpoint. The foundation is the same, but what changed is the training on top of it. Cursor says 2.5 is better at long-running agentic tasks, follows complex instructions more reliably, and handles sustained work better. The benchmarks are up the board, but two things they called out specifically that are not on the benchmarks are communication style and effort collaboration. Effort collaboration means the model adjusts how much work it does based on how hard the task actually is. Composer 2 would sometimes do a lot of reasoning in two calls on a simple request. 2.5 is supposed to do less work on easy tasks and more work on harder ones. Cursor published effort curves in the blog post that show this. The training method is a part worth understanding. They call it targeted RL with textual feedback. Reinforcement learning on long agent run has a problem. The model does a rollout that can be hundreds of thousands of tokens long, and at the end it gets a single reward score for the whole run. If the model made one bad tool call somewhere in the middle, that specific mistake barely affects the final number. So, it's hard to train the model to stop doing that one specific thing. What Cursor did is when they identify a specific moment that the model could have done better, they write a short hint describing the correct behavior and insert it into the context at that exact point. The model with the hint produces better token probabilities at that step. They then train the model without the hint to match those better probabilities. So, they get a targeted training signal at the exact turn where the mistake happened without losing the broader reward over the whole run. items.title: Codex SuperApp: Did OpenAI Just Kill Claude Code?
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: OpenAI just officially built the Codex super app. Three updates dropped today that turn Codex from a desktop coding tool into a full platform running on your Mac, your [music] phone, your CI pipeline, and your company server all at once.
items.transcriptSummary: OpenAI just officially built the Codex super app. Three updates dropped today that turn Codex from a desktop coding tool into a full platform running on your Mac, your [music] phone, your CI pipeline, and your company server all at once.

transcript.text: OpenAI just officially built the Codex super app. Three updates dropped today that turn Codex from a desktop coding tool into a full platform running on your Mac, your [music] phone, your CI pipeline, and your company server all at once. So, let's get into it. OpenAI shipped a Codex update today that on the surface looks like a simple mobile announcement, but it's actually the missing piece in a much bigger play they've been building toward for months, the Codex super app. Let me explain first what exactly is new. Codex is now accessible inside the ChatGPT mobile app on iOS and Android, which is available in every region ChatGPT supports. 4 million people use Codex every week according to OpenAI, and all of them get this as a preview starting today. The mobile app talks to the Codex desktop on your Mac. Windows desktop support is on the road map, but not live yet, which is a real limitation if you're a Windows developer. The pairing happens through what OpenAI calls a secure relay layer, which is the key piece of infrastructure that's powering all of this. The relay matters a lot, so it's worth understanding what it actually is. Normally, if you wanted to access a machine at home from your phone, you'd be using something like SSH with a public IP or running a tunneling service like ngrok or punching a hole through your router. All of those expose your machine to the open internet in some form, and they're a pain to set up. The relay side-steps that entirely. Your Mac connects out to OpenAI's infrastructure, your phone connects out to OpenAI's infrastructure, and the two devices talk through that middle layer. Your machine is never directly reachable from the public web. It also syncs a session state, which means if you start a thread on your laptop, pick it up on your phone, then switch to a different laptop, the context follows you across all three. That is going to be the foundation for a lot of what OpenAI is probably going to ship next. From the phone, you can do everything you would do at the desktop. Review what Codex is working on, approve commands before it runs them, change models mid thread, kick off new task or answer clarifying questions when the agent gets stuck. Real-time updates flow back to your phone including screenshots, terminal output, code diffs and test results. So if Codex is running a test suite back home, you can watch the results land on your phone as they happen. If it makes a code change, you see the diff. If it opens a browser and clicks through a flow, you actually get the screenshot as well. Now here's the part the announcement under plays. Today's update only makes sense if you understand what shipped a month ago on April 16th, which Open AI called Codex for almost everything. That update added native computer use to the Codex Mac app, meaning Codex got the ability to see your screen, control its own cursor, click on UI elements, type into applications and use the clipboard, all inside whatever Mac application you pointed at. It also gained an in-app browser, image generation through GPT image 1.5, memory across sessions and over 90 plugins for connecting to external tools. The way computer use works in Codex is you install a separate computer use plugin inside the app, then grant Mac OS screen recording and accessibility permissions one time. After that, Codex can operate apps with its own cursor without taking over yours, which is a nice feature because it means you can keep working while the agent runs in parallel. It's currently Mac only and excluded from the EU, UK and Switzerland at launch. So when you put today's update together with last ones, you get the full picture. Codex on your Mac can already see your screen, click around, fill out forms, use a browser and run code. As of today, you can trigger all of that from your phone. That's what makes this launch more than just a mobile app launch. The phone becomes a remote control for an AI agent that can operate real software on your real machine. You're not coding on your phone, your phone is just the input layer for an agent that's doing the actual work back home. The other thing that shipped today, which is getting almost no coverage is that remote SSH is now generally available. Codex can connect directly into manage remote development environments, the kind big companies use to give their devs pre-approved dependencies, credentials, and security policies. The desktop app reads your SSH configuration automatically, detects the host you have access to, and lets you spin up Codex projects inside those remote machines just like you would locally. Once you're connected, those remote environments are also reachable from your phone through the same relay infrastructure. This update is geared towards the enterprise market. The biggest blocker for big companies adopting AI agents has been that their code bases sit behind layers of compliance, network restrictions, and credential gating, and most agent products don't fit cleanly inside that. With remote SSH plus the relay, OpenAI is saying your environment stays exactly where it is, locked down, and the agent operates inside it. You don't have to move your code, you don't have to share credentials with the model, you can just point Codex at the box that already has right setup. If you zoom out and ask what this all adds up to, it's not really 4 million people getting a mobile app, it's the assembly of an end-to-end product where the agent runs on your machine, on your company's machine, has the ability to actually use software the way you do, has memory across sessions, and can be steered from anywhere, and lives behind security infrastructure that's enterprise credible. That's the actual product OpenAI has been building piece by piece since February when the Codex desktop app first launched. The competitive context is worth flagging, too. Anthropic has been shipping a very similar shape of product for a while now. Claude has had computer use since October of 2024, and their desktop agent product has had mobile task dispatch for months. So, OpenAI isn't inventing the category here, they're catching up on the features, but arguably kind of pulling ahead based on what they have shipped. Whoever builds the better orchestration, the better safety layer, and the better ecosystem of tools wins the next phase of AI agents. Today's update tells you OpenAI is taking that race seriously. The trust question is real and worth saying out loud. You're authorizing an AI agent to operate apps and a browser on your real machine, signed into your real account. The agent can do anything you could do in those apps. OpenAI has approval gates for sensitive actions, but you should be deliberate. Don't connect this to a Chrome profile that has your bank logged in. Don't point it at apps with destructive right permissions you haven't thought about. To try it, update both the Codex app on Mac and the ChatGPT app on your phone. Sign in on both with the same account, and your Mac will appear as a connected environment. Windows is coming. Whether you actually need this depends on your workflow, but if you're already a Codex user, the mobile piece is the one that will quietly change how often you use it. The other two things that OpenAI quietly dropped today is two features aimed squarely at developer teams trying to bake Codex into their actual engineering workflow. The first is called hooks, and the second is programmatic access tokens. Both are small on their own, but together, they're how Codex starts living inside CI pipelines and internal tooling instead of just on someone's laptop. Hooks let you customize the Codex loop with scripts that fire at specific points in a task. So, you can run a validator before or after Codex does work, scan prompts for secrets before they hit the model, log every conversation to your internal system for compliance, or create memories and customize behavior on a per repo or per directory basis. The last one is the most underrated piece. It means your front end team can have Codex behaving one way inside the repo, and your back end team can have it behaving differently inside theirs. And you don't need to retrain anyone or maintain global configurations. The behavior travels with the code base. Programmatic access tokens are the other piece. These are scoped credentials for business and enterprise teams. You can generate them inside your ChatGPT workspace settings, plug them into CI, release workflows, or internal automations, then now your build pipeline can talk to Codex without anyone having to sign in interactively. You can set expirations on the tokens, revoke them whenever you need, and all the usage stays tied back to the workspace, so you actually have audit logs of what was done with which credentials. If you ever tried to integrate AI into an actual production engineering process, you know that interactive sign-ins are a deal breaker. Pipelines need credentials that machines can use, and admins need to be able to kill those credentials fast if something goes wrong. OpenAI just shipped both. Combined with hooks, this turns Codex into something you can actually wire into your release process without wiring a bunch of glue code. It which is a quiet release, but if you run an engineering team, this is the one you are going to pay attention to. But, that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel, follow our new newsletter as well at universeofai.beehive.com, as well as subscribe to the main channel World of AI, and support us on X as well. Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: Running Local AI on AMD
items.author: Sam Witteveen
items.summary: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge.
items.transcriptSummary: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge.

transcript.text: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge. That gap is shrinking, not growing. Meanwhile, if you look at the frontier labs, tokens look like they're getting cheaper on paper, but practically people's bills are going up because agents and reasoning eat tokens at a completely different scale than chat ever did. And then the other thing that's becoming totally clear is that people really want to have privacy and control over the AI that they're using. And so while there are these amazing frontier models that are coming, the big question seems to be is who's going to be able to afford to use them at scale. And more and more local AI is just starting to make more sense for a lot of the workloads out there. So the question I've been looking at more and more lately is what actual hardware stacks can get you there. And in this video I want to take a look at the AMD side of that. So I've teamed up with Zidex and AMD to get access to one of their workstations with a Threadripper 9980X and an AMD Radeon AI Pro R9 700 GPU with 32 gig of VRAM. I got to say at the start the results I'm seeing are really impressive and have actually surprised me on what can actually be done now with this kind of system. All right, so let me unpack again why local. The open weight models that we're seeing now, the Qwen 3.6, the Gemma, the Kimmy, the Deep Seek models, etc. These aren't toys. You can do real work with them. I've been covering many of them on my channel and if you've been following the open space at all, you can feel that this trend is not slowing down. While it's clear that the top three frontier labs do have an advantage, Open models have caught up and surpassed a lot of the second-tier labs that are still trying to position themselves at the frontier. But, the biggest factor that I'm seeing is tokens. And this falls into sort of two parts. The first part is that as these models get so good, people want to be able to run them on their own machine doing things for things like coding agents, for personal agents like Open Claw, like Hermes agent, etc. And those particular tasks because they're using reasoning and because they're using agentic calls, they are burning through tokens. If you're paying for a frontier lab model for that, you're going to find that you better have a decent budget. The days of the all-you-can-eat plans are are being dramatically scaled back so that they're only supporting their own particular coding agents, etc. And as we're seeing the next generation of models being talked about, it's pretty clear that those are going to cost a lot more than what the current top-tier models are going to cost. So, this combined with wanting to basically control your data means that you have to look at local AI solutions. All right.
