items.title: Managed Agents: The Biggest Shift in AI Development Right Now
items.author: Prompt Engineering
items.summary: Let's talk about managed agents because they are going to be a big trend in 2026. So, Google shipped their manage agents in Gemini API as a part of their anti-gravity 2.0.
items.transcriptSummary: Let's talk about managed agents because they are going to be a big trend in 2026. So, Google shipped their manage agents in Gemini API as a part of their anti-gravity 2.0.

transcript.text: Let's talk about managed agents because they are going to be a big trend in 2026. So, Google shipped their manage agents in Gemini API as a part of their anti-gravity 2.0. This comes after anthropic released the first version of their manage agents and they have been aggressively updating their version. Now agents have moved from single turn responses to long horizon task that run for minutes or hours. So if you're still rolling out your own agent loops for that kind of work, this is the pattern you need to pay attention to because the entire industry is converging on it. Now in this video we are going to walk through what exactly manage agents are. what is the pain point they're solving where Google Anthropic implementations are different and why this whole category matters if you're building agents. Okay, so let me break it down. So a manage agent is a hosted runtime that runs the agent loop for you. So you describe the agent which is basically the model, the system prompt and the tools and then you send messages. The platform handles everything else for you. Now that everything else is the part that takes the most time. The longer the agent runs, the harder all of this gets. You are spinning up a sandbox to execute code persist state across tool calls and you have to manage context windows. Also you need to think about handling failures and retries along with oath refresh and network egress sometimes even container isolation. Now the model and uh tool definition is the easiest part. The rest of the orchestration is what actually matters the most. So a manage agent moves all of this to the provider sandbox. They manage the session state for you including recovery logic and you get a stream of events and the final output. Now here's how the uh way enthropic frames it and I think it's a useful way to think about this category in general. They break the agent into three different pieces. The brain is the model and it's decision loop. Now it's stateless which mean it can be rebooted without losing the session. Then you have the hands which are the sandbox and tools. These are affirmal and disposable. When one fails, the platform just spins up a new one and the session keeps going. Now, the session is durable appendon log of what exactly is happening. The most important thing is that it lives outside the model's context window. If the brain crashes, you wake a new brain, hand it the session, and it's going to pick up from the last recorded event. Now this decoupling of all these three elements is critical when it comes to managed agent design from entropic because this is what makes the runtime resilient especially for long running workloads. And what makes a manage agent a real product category and not just a feature is because what the providers are shipping now it's not the model anymore. it's going to be the runtime and the product that you build on top of this model and that's where manage agent comes into play. Okay, so let me actually walk you through what those pin points looks like in practice. So let's start with code execution. Your agent needs to run scripts, say bash commands, Python, sometimes whatever the agent decides to write. Now that code runs against your own infrastructure unless you sandbox it. Now here are the things that you will need. You need a container per session, a file system that agent can read and write, package manager, sometimes a network policy, and a way to kill the whole thing cleanly when the session ends. Now the next thing is persistent state for these long running agents. A longunning agent is not just a single API call. It's a loop. Within this loop, the model decides which tool to call. The results are going to come back and the model is going to make a decision again. Now, imagine if the loop crashes halfway through. You want to resume it from where it left off, not start over. So, you need an appendon event log that lives outside the models context window and survive container restarts. The main way to think about is separation of concerns and you want to treat these things independently. Now for agents there are also credentials. If the agent is acting on behalf of the user, say they're posting to their Slack or reading their GitHubs, you need oath tokens, a way to refresh them when they expire and a way to keep them out of the sandbox. Otherwise, a prompt ejection can leak them. Also, you need to think about agent identities the way you would think about user identity identities. Now, something like this is going to take weeks to build properly and months to harden it. Now, the fourth thing to think about is recovery from failures. The longer the sessions run, the more chances for something to go wrong. Think about things about like network blips, a container going out of memory or with the current generation just hitting a rate limit. So if your harness lives in the memory, one failure is going to kill the whole session. Now the fix is to make the session stateless and push the state into the event log. And this is a really new way of thinking about the architecture. It's not just a configuration tweak anymore. And here's the thing, none of this is the interesting part of building an agent. All of this is infrastructure. And that's exactly why providers are absorbing it because the infrastructure is going to keep evolving with your agents. And you don't have to worry about that part anymore. Now, every company is building the same plumbing. So, these agent providers are building these manage agents which provide you with the plumbing. Okay? So, both Entropic and Google Shift manage agents. But the implementations are very different. That's what I want to talk about in the rest of the video. But before that, these videos takes a lot of time and effort and somebody has to pay the bills. So, a quick word from today's sponsor. A quick note before we keep going. I want to show you N8N. It's a platform for building automation workflows. You wire up nodes. Each node does a specific thing and the whole thing runs end to end. You can run it on nan cloud or self-hosted. They recently released their own MCP server which enables you to create and edit workflows from any coding agent. Once you configure the MCP server, you can use something like /go for the agent to iteratively refine the workflow and test it. Now for this to work you will have to enable instance level MCP in your settings. Okay. So in this example I'm going to be using cloud code. I have already connected the N8 MCP server. Now here I am asking Claude to build me an NAN workflow that takes a topic from web hook post body fetches recent papers on archive and recent stories on hacker news and trending repos from GitHub in parallel and then run an AI agent that synthesize a brief summary. So now we're going to send this in and Claude is going to build the whole workflow for us just using that MCP server. Now here's the workflow that it generated for us. All of these nodes were connected by cloud and we can ask it to validate this test and validate the workflow for us. And seems like all of the nodes ran successfully. We can actually see what response had it generated based on the request that was provided. And here is what we got from the agent. Now during the testing it found that there is a rate limit on archive X. So now the agent through the MCP server can keep updating the workflow until it works. At the moment we are posting a request which means that you can integrate this into any application and trigger the workflow from anywhere. Let's say I want persistence. So after every API call, I wanted to keep the research history and this table with the brief itself. Now all we need to do is just to tell Claude to implement this for us and it's going to go back and edit the workflow. So the query ran successfully. We can look at the data. So here's the query, here's the summary and then we have the items or columns that we ask it to store. Now this is going to persist across sessions. Now since this is running on N10 cloud, you can trigger it from anywhere within any application. If you want to try this yourself, the N10 cloud free trial link is in the description with a coupon for a free month. The workflow template is also linked so you can install it in your own instance in one click. Thanks to NA10 for making this video possible through their sponsorship. Now back to the video. So both of them have manage agents but the implementations are very different. Let me walk you through four places the gap shows up the most. Let's uh start with the API. So with Enthropic you can create an agent, you create an environment, you create a session and then you send events into that specific session. So you have four resources with four different endpoints. Now the beauty is that these are reusable and the agent itself is versioned. So you can roll updates into it. The session streams typed events back to you. So something like agent dot message agent tool use agent status and so on and you can interrupt midrun. You can steer the agent with new messages. You can update tools or MCP servers between runs. Now with Google it's just a single call. You call the interactions.create with an agent ID and input and an environment parameter. The agent plans, executes, tool results are observed and then loops until the whole loop is done and you get the final output. Now, if you want to reuse the same sandbox, you pass the same environment ID or the next call. That's kind of the whole contract here. So, Enthropic gives you more knobs to control. Google is going for very simplistic architecture where you don't really have much control. So these are same ideas but opposite bets. Now the tools is where the next big gap is actually present at the moment. Keep in mind Google's version is just a week old. Entropic is about 5 6 weeks old by now. Okay. So Entropic ships the full pre-built tool set. On the back end, it has bash, file operations, web search, web fetch with a first class MCP, plus custom tools you define and execute on your client, plus a pre-built skills for things like Excel and PDF. Now, on top of that, they have Walts, which is per user oath credentials that Enthropic manages and refreshes for you. Now, Google's integrity agent ships code execution, Google search, URL fetch, and file system. That's pretty much it. Uh, at the moment, there's no MCP for this agent, no custom function calling, no WS, a bunch of things are missing. But hold that thought because there is a wrinkle here. But there's another version which has some really interesting details. that I'm going to look at in a minute. Now, today Anthropic is really shipping the whole ecosystem around manage agents. Google is just starting right now. Okay. So, on top of the basic loop, Enthropic ships three things that Google doesn't have yet. So, the first one is memory stores which persist across sessions and version every right. Then you have outcomes which lets you give the agent a rubric. The platform spins up a separate grader that stores the work and tells the agent to iterate. And then there is dream which is an async job that reads past sessions and consolidates the memory into cleaner store. Now these are some really interesting ideas that I think will get incorporated into other managed systems as well. Okay, next we're going to look at where it runs and what it costs because in both cases these companies are managing the infrastructure for you. So Entropic gives you a hosted cloud container and then a selfhosted sandbox option where orchestration stays on Entropic but tool execution moves to your infrastructure. Now that matters if you have data residency or compliance constraints. Uh at the moment Google is cloud only there are no self-hosted options at the moment. Now on pricing anthropic charges standard token rate plus 8 cents per active session hour. Google charges token rates only. The sandbox compute is free during the preview. Okay. So this looks like a simpler build on paper, but aentic loops can run for a long time and can burn through millions of tokens. So the actual task or the actual cost per run depends heavily on what the agent is doing. So everything I said so far applies to Google's consumer anti-gravity agent, the one that you can directly access through Gemini API. But Google actually ships a second manage agent product on Gemini enterprise agent platform which uses the same harness underneath which is the anti-gravity harness but the feature set is much closer to what Anthropic offers and I found this by reading through the documentation it wasn't really mentioned with the official release. Now it adds MCP server support. It also adds oath based o manager. It adds memory bank which works a lot like anthropics memory stores and it also adds a skill registry for custom skills and there's also supposed to be an agent to agent framework for multi- aent coordination. Now the catch is that this is in private preview right now. It means it's preg Google explicitly says don't use it with your confidential data yet. So we have the same agent definition move between the two. So if you want a feature parity with anthropic today the enterprise tier is actually where it lives. The Gemini API version is the simpler product most developers will start with. Okay. So to add it up, you have got two products solving the same problem with two very different philosophies. We will have to see which one is going to be the winner. But more importantly, what exactly does this mean for you as a user? Okay, so let's step back from the API details for a minute. The real meat of this comparison is not the feature list. It's that Enthropic and Google are placing very different bets on what a manage agent should look like. Now let's unpack that based on my current understanding. So Anthropic's bet is really on depth. You design an agent as a versioned resource, attach it to an environment, run sessions against it and stream events back with every concept has its own endpoint. So the platform exposes the machinery in this case whether those are credentials, memory stores, outcomes, multi- aent coordination. Everything separately is a first class resource that you compose. Now the price you pay is basically you need to learn more concepts. However, this is the only product on the market with per user oath vaults cross session memories that are versioned. Now, they're treating the agent runtime like an operating system by virtualizing components, exposing stable interfaces and absorbing the entire operations layer so you don't have to. Now, Google's bet on the other hand is simplicity. You hit interaction.create create with an agent ID, an input, and an environment. The harness does everything in a single autonomous loop. There are no separate sessions environment persist by ID, but the call is just this single unit. Now, customization in this case is marked on files, not JSON. Now, the price you pay is that whole categories of capabilities that do not exist yet on their platform. Uh at the moment there is no MCP, no custom tools, no vaults for storing credentials and no memory stores. Now most of these are going to be available in the enterprise tier that we talked about earlier, but that's still prega. Okay, so here's the market read in my opinion. Enthropicus is competing on depth of agent runtime. They want to absorb the entire agent ops layer and make every one of those first class primitives in their API. Google is easily competing on simplicity. Both API and the models are designed to feel light. The flash model runs roughly four times faster than other Frontier models, which matters when the agent is running long loops. However, pricing is more complicated than the headlines suggest. Per token flash is cheaper than Opus, but Gemini 3.5 flash is several times the per token price of previous flash generation. And these agentic loops can burn through 3 to 5 million tokens in a single run. So, Google's own talk flag uh cost hitting around $5 per interaction at that scale. So, cheap per token uh doesn't always mean cheap per run. So, their bet seems to be that speed and simpler API and lower uh per token rates would add to a win even if the real cost per run depends heavily on what the agent does. They want to make a simple single API call good enough that you don't notice it's an agent. Okay. So these are incremental product differences. There are two different theories of where agent value come from. So anthropic is trying to say that runtime is the product. The model is the engine inside. Now Google is kind of saying that the model is fast enough and the API is simple enough that the runtime should disappear into a single call. But what exactly does this mean for you as a builder? If your differentiation is going to come from how the agent works, the tool it has, the credential it carries, the way it iterates toward a goal, Enthropic is the platform built for that today. Google is the opposite bet. It's for builders whose differentiation come from what the agent produces and who want the simplest possible path to ship today. Now the biggest thing to think about and most teams underestimate this is vendor lock in in both the obvious and less obvious sense. So the obvious sense is that Anthropics API is not going to work with Gemini and vice versa. If you commit, you're committing to the provider's road map, their pricing, their rate limits, their availability. The less obvious sense is the one that actually breaks production systems. These are non-deterministic systems to begin with and on top of that even within a single provider the underlying model is going to shift on you. The Frontier Labs uh update system prompts under the hood, quantize models to run them faster and cheaper, allegedly, retune behavior on safety, and other cycles that you never see. Now, each one of those can change how the agent is going to act, sometimes in ways the lab themselves do not anticipate. We have seen this play out repeatedly. A lab ships an optimized version of the model for cost or speed. And the downstream of this is that there's a measurable regression. Tool calls gets worse. Reasoning chain gets shorter. Certain task starts failing the way they did not last week. Now the problem is none of this is in the change logs. you find out because your evol starts drifting or you start getting complaints from your users. So when you build on manage agent platform, you're accepting more than API lockins. You're accepting that the underlying behavior is going to drift and you don't control when and how. So build with that in mind. Invest in evaluation harness. track outputs over time and don't bake assumption about the model behavior into parts of your system that matters. Okay, the other trade-offs are quicker. So both products are stateful by design which means they are not eligible for zero data retention or hip-hop business associate agreements today and both are still in preview or beta. the pricing and feature set can shift. But the bigger point is that uh this is becoming the default way frontier providers ship agent capabilities. Enthropic shift first, Google followed AWS and OpenAI are right there with their own versions. If you're building anything that runs longer than a single API call, the next question is going to be are you running it or is your provider? If you're building agents, let me know in the comments which way you are going or Google Simplicity or rolling your own. Thanks for watching and as always, I'll see you in the next one.

Um what we're seeing coming out now is much more sort of influenced by DeepMind you know, with the the new agent stuff than it than perhaps ADK originally was. Maybe ADK's changed a bit now, but um yeah, it's it's interesting to sort of see I think it affects the framework companies a lot. You know, recently on VentureBeat we interviewed Jerry Liu from LlamaIndex. He kind of called that like in some ways the agent frameworks are over, you know, that nowadays you can get pretty much any coding agent to write a nice harness or write some kind of, you know, code that you want for your, um, particular agent. And then, really, it's more now about like, how do you host that? Mhm. How do you hook that up to some kind of harness? Uh, how do you have have a sandbox, as you said, have some observability logging. Um, in many of the cases, how if it crashes, how does it automatically restart? Things like that. Okay, so you touched upon uh, frameworks. Uh, I already agree with you that probably, uh, you don't need frameworks anymore, right? But we are now going into this era of harness engineering. Do you think a year from now, when we are at IO again, uh, we're going to see that the harness companies, or like these third-party harnesses, were also not something viable? So, this is this really good question. Um, so at at Cloud Next, you and I walked around and talked to some of these these companies. And you were a bit more aggressive than I was, I will say, [laughter] in challenging them, saying, "Uh, you guys are in trouble, because I won't say the swear word, but you guys are in trouble, because, uh, you know, you guys don't control the model." And I think at the time, it made a lot of sense. Since then, I've spent some time talking to the people at Anthropic, Mhm. and realizing, you know, when you talk to the people at Anthropic and say, "Hey, so how do you guys use the cheaper models like Haiku and stuff like that in your everyday?" And it's very quickly it's like, "Um, uh, uh, we just use Opus 4.7 on X high, right?" Yeah. >> [laughter] >> Uh, and then you realize, okay, these big companies can't afford that, right? They just don't have they We're we're really now at the point where people are going to have to become much more And and they talked about, you know, Sundar even talked about this today in the keynote, that uh, they were saying, I think a lot of their enterprise customers are doing a trillion tokens >> per day. per day, right? >> Um, that's insane, right? You know, if you think about that. So, if if they've got things like that and that that kind of explains [clears throat] where all the chippies have gone, by the way. If you If you've been [laughter] trying to use anti-gravity or anything and you couldn't get quota, now you know where it's gone, >> [laughter] >> right? Um Anyway, that like I I think maybe some of maybe some of those those companies like Factory AI and stuff like that that we spoke to who actually did have interesting arguments, right? Could actually turn out to be true that that if you, you know, if you want to use Opus 4.7 for, you know, a lot of things and then you want to use 3.5 flash for some things and then flash light for a large bulk of it, you're you're probably not going to be able to do that with the managed agents from individual providers. So, we will see, you know, like I actually you possibly will be able to do it with Google because Google's got access to all the Anthropic models, they sell them. They won't be able to do it with like the Open AI models perhaps at the moment. Um but do you think like there would be application-specific custom harnesses? Uh for sure, yeah. >> Right. So, I I feel like that probably is a trend that we're going to see like >> we've talked about. You also agreed that with that, yeah. >> Yeah, yeah. I totally agree with that, right? So, okay, uh now let's talk about the models themselves, right? Uh I think we are seeing more and more capable models. Where do you see the uh scope for open-source models? I kind of have a controversial opinion on that. I want to hear yours first. Okay, so I've been getting back into local AI a lot more since Gemma 4 came out. Uh And and the latest sort of Quen models, the latest Gemma 4 models and stuff like that. Um I do think it is amazing how good these these local models are getting. Mhm. And if you project out 6 months from now, a year from now, you've got to think that we have open models that are just as good as the models that we're currently using that are proprietary models, probably even better. Mhm. I And at at some point, it's like how much intelligence do you need, right? You know, if if you if you're hiring for a customer service uh you know, person, you don't need the person with 200 IQ, right? You can you you know, hey, 120 IQ is great, right? Or whatever. Um and I think like that's going to be I think that's where where things are going. And so, as we get things where these models uh you know, as the models get better on one thing, also we're starting to see new kinds of memory come onto the market. Uh you know, I think this is an area that I can't talk about too much, [laughter] but we're going to see a lot [clears throat] of things come onto the market um that actually make it so that your phone can run a 30 40 50 billion parameter model, maybe as a MOE with only 3 4 billion active, but that will uh certainly become something that I radically changes things, right? If you've got a model that good uh to do that that can do customer service in a heartbeat, right? If if especially if it's post-trained to do function calling, skill use, sandbox use, these sorts of things. Uh it's it's going to be on par with what are some of the proprietary models today. So, I I think that you know, >> Interesting. Okay. What's your What's your take? >> All right. I I would personally divide them into three different tiers, right? So, smaller models, yes, those are probably user-focused. You could run them. Uh then mid-size models like 30 32 B and things like that, right? Those are probably more for, uh, small business applications, more, um, specialized workflows, right? And then you have the third, like the bigger 1 trillion parameter models, right? Those are probably not, uh, like, even though if you call them open weight, right? Those are not for people like us, right? Yeah, right. So, you you need to have like enterprise grade, uh, hardware to actually run those, right? And now there are like third-party services which host these models, right? So, if you're hitting an API endpoint, I would rather hit a Google API endpoint or OpenAI API endpoint, right? Rather than like, uh, Kimera or DeepSeek. Okay, I I I totally get the argument for not wanting to hit a Chinese or, you know, or endpoint in a certain country and stuff like that. Um, we've even seen that, maybe not with Kimera, I think are are really cool. Uh, Deep Seek, I think is really cool. But there's some of the other ones, I won't name names, that supposedly are just collecting up all of this data to train on, right? Uh, and and I'm and actually maybe you're even being quite honest about it, more so than other companies. Um, so yeah, I think like things like that. But the cool thing is like I while for most people, okay, anyone sitting in their bedroom is not going to run an eight-node, you know, uh, eight H200s or something like that, right? That's beyond most people. But that's not beyond people running on prem in their company, right? Where they've got basically, you know, if you've got if you've got a a uh, a a bank or some other large sort of organization, it's not a big deal for you to spend that money to have that kind of node to be able to concurrently support 30, 40, 50, 100 users at the same time, so that probably means you can support out for like at least a thousand, you know, people. Okay, so in that case, right, what is the incentive for the model creators? Like how does the open source economy work? All right. So, look, I think at some point, we've already seen it with Quen, but they pulled back. Yeah. And then they were kind of forced to release, right? That they kind of were saying, you know, >> [laughter] >> um that they didn't want to make, you know, the open models as much. Uh and then, you know, perhaps some people like myself said, "Well, I'm not going to make a video." >> [laughter] >> And then they were like, "Oh, well, you know, turned out maybe not because of me, I'm certainly not saying that, but because, you know, because there was pushback from a lot of people, they decided to make some of these models open. Um look, I think at the end of the day, there will always be a lot of users that want to know, "Hey, I can fine-tune this version of this model, and I can stick it in the cloud, and then we can, you know, we don't want to we want to be hands-off, we don't want to have something like that." But for like a bank, for a hospital where they want a model on prem or somewhere that they can control every single thing about it for privacy reasons, those kind of things, they will happily go down that direction. >> But kind of uh exploring that topic more, do you think these companies who are releasing open weight models, they're going to have more strict licenses >> It could be, yes. Like that could be that could be the case, right? You know, we could see uh so remember there was a big uproar around the Cursor 2 model, >> Yeah. which turned out that actually was kind of a bit dumb, right? Because actually they had licensed it, they, you know, they licensed the right from Kimi to be able to fine-tune it. Actually, everything had been done, except they just didn't tell people that in their blog post cuz they wanted to, you know, sound like they made it the whole thing themselves. And so that one was I thought was very funny because you had people uh you know, on the Kimi side or the Kimi team or whatever, quite pissed off about this. And [laughter] I sort of felt rightly so, you know, tweeting out about it. And then all of a sudden all their tweets got boom. >> [laughter] >> Right? And clearly someone at Kimi had said, "No, no, no, they actually negotiated a license with us. We're making some money. This is the partner, you know, this is how it's all going down." I And then Kimi was forced to come out and say, "Well, actually, yeah, it is a fine-tune or something." >> Okay, that's an interesting point. And I think like one thing I would say I wanted to highlight was that Kimi K2 fine-tuning and RL shows that I think there is a lot more we can extract from RL environment and good data with computer, right? So, maybe like we don't need that many Well, we we definitely need like um architectural innovations, right? But still seems like there is a lot more we can extract from the existing architectures. Yeah, um you got to be careful what we say. All right, yeah, look, I I agree totally, yes. I think, you know, that it is amazing. So, uh it is amazing that the uh the the sort of bigger, you know, the the 2B Gemma 4 Mhm. is beating the the 27B from the generation before, right? >> Yeah. And then and then the the bigger Gemma 4 models are beating the previous generations of Gemini Flashlight and Gemma, you know, and some of the other models from before and stuff like that, right? So, uh Yes, you know, we can uh definitely people are able to work out how to take a lot of that intelligence and put it into a smaller form factor, right? That that's been lots of different tricks. Obviously, the RL environment is one of them. Um you know, just just the way that those new small Gemma 4 models have got per layer embeddings, which is a really cool trick that works for small models, doesn't work that you don't need it for bigger models. Once you get beyond a certain size, it doesn't really help you at all. Um but for small models, awesome, right? And it means that they can just you can run them on an Android phone. They can sort of put the different uh embeddings around in different parts of RAM and stuff like that, and it just all works really nicely. I And I think like those are the kind of tricks that yes, we're seeing, you know, these models get better and better and better. I personally I kind of feel like we have to get to some point where you're just like hey, uh I don't really know the difference between this model and this model anymore. In fact, I think we're already at that with, you know, some proprietary models. The frontier lines, right? >> Um you asked me about we, you know, about Gemini 3.5 Flash. You know, it's a nice model. Yes, very nice model. I tried it out with different things. It worked out, you know. I I didn't make a video about it because like, it's like, okay, what am I going to show that this is hugely better than the one before? Yeah, okay, it's very much vibes at the moment, right? You know, like I I I still haven't seen all the benchmarks for it. I, you know, the artificial analysis should be really interesting to see. You know, I think you were saying that they're saying it uses 5x more tokens. >> Yeah, so like I think it uses 5x more token to uh I I need to be careful here because I don't exactly remember. I just I think like for the same benchmark it uses 5x more tokens, right? For pricing wise, it's 3x more expensive both for input and for output. Okay, I haven't looked at the pricing yet. So, okay. The And yeah, I guess we should preface this with we're literally recording this just after this has been announced. So, it we may have gotten some of the facts wrong. Um but definitely yes, my vibe is that, you know, it it's it's using more tokens, uh you know. And then so then you got to factor in well okay if it's faster it's faster but hey if it's using more tokens then is it really faster to get to the answer you know what what is it that I care about do I care about the speed of tokens per second do I care about time to first token do I care about time to full answer. Yeah. Right if it's time to full answer then maybe it's actually you know for certain things it's not going to be a win but for a lot of things it will be does seem like for anti-gravity and stuff like that you know they they talked about that they made a whole OS with this. For 2.6 Trillion? Billion? >> Billion billion yes 2.6 billion tokens right. >> a token. >> about yeah 1,000 bucks for for making the OS. I don't really know what the OS can do that's any better than Linux or something that that's more what I was kind of interested like if you showed me hey we made this OS that's just for genetic data. You know and I think we will see things like that right I think we saw you know Demis talking about you know isomorphic and and what's going on there and I think that's where you will see the some of the real interesting things with these things where they do actually make things that really couldn't exist before or would have been just too cost uh prohibitive to do that kind of thing before. Okay so do you think in your opinion uh these Frontier Labs are now trying to focus more on the inference and trying to reduce the cost rather than like the uh making bigger models like if we we see a trend that okay we are seeing consolidation in capabilities and I think it's more about applications now right? I I think okay a few things uh there I think definitely the days of just make the model bigger are over right um the days of just train on more tokens is still going on. >> Yeah I agree. Right and and now the tokens that were the amount of tokens for pre pre-training versus the tokens you know it used to be like pre-training was this and then post-training was like this tiny little bit over here. I that's changed, right? Uh so yes, overall a lot more tokens, but also the splits of how that you know is going on is clearly changed. Um so I think yeah, okay that that's one thing. Um I think the labs all the labs want more intelligent models. Mhm. Right? I I think just the the drive of Demis, of Dario, of the people at OpenAI uh um is that they want to reach AGI and they all believe that this AGI thing which no one you know which anyway we we shouldn't even get into that, right? [laughter] Um they all believe this AGI thing is going to come with a smarter model and and that's going to happen. I at the same time they're all getting you know they're all realizing hey, we can make money out of this stuff now and that lots of people want to buy tokens. So how do we make it more efficient to to you know create those tokens, serve those tokens, collect money for those tokens. And I think so you've got this this playing off. Google's a big enough lab that they've always got people working on many sides of it. They've got the Pareto frontier going that they're constantly trying to push. I think Anthropic kind of similar, perhaps a little bit more weighted to the the top end intelligence in what they're focused on. I OpenAI, I don't know, right? Like you know they seem to go back and forth that like the the current 5.5 seems definitely a step forward in the intelligence sort of way. Um will we see smaller models? You know you you you told me a really interesting stat before about Arc-EGI. Do you want to tell people that about like the X5 versus not X5 for 3.5? That was going to be interesting. >> This really interesting. Uh I might get the wrong numbers wrong, but in the morning when I saw on RKG A2 um flash 3.5 scores, I think 8% with minimal thinking. >> minimal thinking. >> Right. And then it goes to I think 90% with high, which is kind of incredible, right? And I think it for RKG A2 it's it's RKG A3 it's very similar, right? Seems like within the same model just by changing the token the reasoning token budget, you can extract a lot more performance. It's kind of incredible. In In some ways it's not surprising, right? If you if you basically allow the model to generate more tokens, to go down different paths, to pull them back, uh it it's going to do better. We know for example that deep think and stuff like that will do that in parallel and can be extremely slow to do it, but can actually find a lot of interesting paths that perhaps the certainly that that the sort of no thinking will the ones will find. Like earlier, I remember that there were some a few studies in which uh especially with the earlier reasoning models, they showed that if the model uh stops reasoning early, they tend to generate correct responses. If it thinks for longer after certain point, like it usually tends to generate wrong answers, right? I I think a lot of that research though is very hard to judge because people are constantly trying to improve how the chain of thought works. So, if you've got like these step-by-step chain of thought, Mhm. uh we're seeing with GPT 5.5, they're trying to scale it back, so instead of n number of steps, it's now n minus five, you know, number of steps. Interesting. >> and and you know, and and the challenge is that those papers that test those things test one version of this, yeah, and they don't necessarily see all the improvements over time or something like that. So, it's not that the paper's wrong, it's just that the paper is like a snapshot of one particular way of rolling out these long chain of thoughts. Okay. Uh I think we're coming up on time. >> Uh let's see. If I were to ask you today, who do you think is winning the the AI race? You can't ask me that. You can't ask me that. Um >> [laughter] >> okay. Uh who's winning the AI race? Okay, I think in general humans are, right? That Okay, I will take the cop-out answer. >> [laughter] >> I think No, I think like I you know, I I find this kind of insane cuz I I started working with LLMs in 2018, all right, maybe even 2017, something like that, playing around with them. And even back then they were, you know, the ideas were kind of cool and stuff like that. Like I Okay, going back to LSTMs, I remember like with LSTMs, right, we had it you know, we had days, right? We we we we would we would make an LLM a a language model with it with LSTMs, and it could go out maybe sort of steps, right? With the whole sort of char RNN sort of days and stuff like that. And and back then we were just like, "Oh, if we could just get 10x this, right?" And then we got to like a basic attention, and it could go out 150 steps, something. Then finally the transformer came along, BERT came along, you could go to 500s, you know, stuff. And that was like, "My god, mind-blowing, you know, this thing can suddenly do stuff." Okay, now forget the size of the context window. It can just do stuff, you know, so much better. So, you know, I I think ideas that just that were not even not only just impossible, but even on sort of impossible to think about back then, suddenly now are just like, "Oh, yeah, that could work, right?" Or that's an interesting idea, you know, that these things could work. And so I so I think like, you know, who's winning? I think we are, right? I'll say that, right? That like you know, anyone who's using the models, and especially if you played with, you know, some of the early models, even people who played with GPT-3, you know, are kind of blown away by the change. >> as a practitioner, yes, like even if 5 years ago you would have asked me what these things would be possible, I would say like no. >> Yeah, no, no, no, exactly. That That That kind of crazy. All right, we are almost on time. Uh any parting thoughts? Um Not really. Uh you know, I I guess look, we we haven't touched on the manage agent stuff. Some really interesting stuff coming there. Uh maybe at some point we do a follow-up or something like that. We We We will definitely uh make videos about some of this stuff. It It, you know, you want to stay on top of it, I kind of feel. Like you definitely want to be able to check out the thing. And I think you've got to also move to the the sort of point where you start saying, "Okay, I don't care what Sam the YouTuber says about this model. I'm going to test it on my e-vals, right? I'm going to test it on my use case." Cuz maybe for me it's really awesome, but for for this person it's not. Like, you know, those sorts of things I think you need to really focus on that nowadays much more than uh than like just, "Oh, it's it the model's this much better or 10% better or something like that." It's not about those things. It's such We're We're dealing with such jagged intelligence now that the model can be, you know, a genius many times over in one area and like dumber than a 5-year-old kid in other areas. That's perfectly put. We're going to stop on that. Thanks for watching everybody. Me and Sam signing out. Thanks. Bye, everyone. Bye, everyone.

items.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash is the Best Google Model Yet?
items.author: Prompt Engineering
items.summary: Okay, so Google is releasing Gemini 1.5 flash today. And this is a bigger deal than most people realize.
items.transcriptSummary: Okay, so Google is releasing Gemini 1.5 flash today. And this is a bigger deal than most people realize.

transcript.text: Okay, so Google is releasing Gemini 1.5 flash today. And this is a bigger deal than most people realize. This is the first Gemini model that is going GA at release in a while. That means you'll be able to use this model in production starting today. This hasn't happened before. Also, this gives you 3.1 Pro preview level performance at the cost of a flash model. Now, the price has increased. However, flash plays a very critical role when it comes to Google's Gemini AI strategy. This is what we would call a workhorse model, a model that does most of the work in an agentic workflow. And data actually shows this. There's a recent study from Marcel that shows that Gemini 3 flash is one of the most widely used model for agentic workflows. Data from Open Router also shows that the flash models are one of the most widely used models that are powering agentic systems in production environments. So, when Google releases flash model, you have to pay attention to it, especially with this one, because it gives you the pro level performance based on my own tests. Now, Google DeepMind was kind enough to give me early access, so I have been playing around with the model for quite a while. In this video, I'm going to be showing you some of the examples and test cases. Uh at the time of the recording of this video, I don't have access to the benchmarks. So, this video is going to be mostly based on my own experience without being biased by what the benchmark says. So, let's have a look at all the tests that I was able to run. I will see you on the other side. So, according to GDM, this model is really good at agentic coding, and I wanted to see how good this is going to be at building complex project that goes beyond a single shot implementation. So, this is a project that I saw on X where they're building an airflow simulator for jet stream. Now, there are a number of different implementations of this or at least tweets about it. So, the one that I'm using is this and they initially used image two model from OpenAI and then used Gemini 3.1 Pro to create the simulation. But, in my case, I took that video, I put it into a language model and generated a text description of this. And this is what I used to create the version that you're seeing right now. Now, this was built using the build feature within AI studio. So, it's not a single shot implementation. It's actually a lot more complex with a multi-agent setup which is powered by Google anti-gravity. And the result actually is pretty good. Normally, the demos that you're going to see for these new models usually single shot prompts, but the actual use is going to be a lot more complex. So, these single shot prompts are not going to be good representative of what the model is actually capable of. And that's kind of what we want to explore with this specific project because it is very complex with a lot of different moving parts. Now, the rest of the prompts that I'm going to be testing are going to be single shot because usually, if I were to implement something like this for every test, that's going to take a lot of time. But, here's how this whole setup works. You can do or set up the throttle settings for the project and and this screen is actually going to show you the corresponding changes in the simulation. So, it can build and simulate pretty complex systems and that's kind of the beauty of this model, because it is able to give you frontier level performance at relatively lower cost. In the rest of the video, I want to show you a direct comparison between this new Flash 3.5 model and also a 3.1 Pro preview. But we're going to start off with a quick comparison of a 3 Flash preview. Okay, so this model is way different from the general Flash model that Google have released so far. So to show you, let's run a live test. In this case, I want it to create an animation of a crowd of people walking to form "Hello world, I'm Gemini." And we want it to use 3.js. On the left-hand side, we have Gemini 3 Flash preview. On the right-hand side, we have Gemini 3.5 Flash. The very first thing that you're going to notice is that Gemini 3.5 Flash is very slow compared to Gemini 3 Flash preview. Now, this could be attributed to that I'm testing that in early access, so there might be some compute constraints. But if you see, Gemini 3 Flash preview has already generated a response in 20 seconds. Whereas Gemini 3.5 is still thinking. It is generating those reasoning tokens. For this test, Gemini 3.5 Flash took about a minute to generate the response. Now, there is another big difference. 3 Flash preview generated 4,400 tokens, whereas 3.5 generated about 21,000 tokens. For 3 Flash preview, the chain of thought is way simplistic and straightforward. It doesn't have much information, whereas the 3.5 Flash chain of thought is a lot more structured and this kind of resembles that of Gemini 3.1 Pro preview. It's a lot more organized. It's way step-by-step and the model thinks about every step that is going to take. Okay, so the first one is the response from the Gemini 3 flash preview. In this case, it starts randomly. However, it's able to actually put everything together in the exact cloud formation that we wanted. And this is actually pretty incredible even for the Gemini 3 flash preview that this model is able to do it because most of the other models that I have tested have a hard time completing this specific prompt. Now, this is the response or output from a Gemini 3.5 flash. There are two different things. One is scattered swarm and then if you click on form text, basically it brings everybody and puts them in the text arrangement that we wanted. It also included a couple of different perspectives. So, one is top view, the other one is perspective view. Very simplistic, but things actually work, which is nice. Now, in the beginning of the video, I said that this is a lot closer to Gemini 3.1 Pro than its flash predecessor. I ran the same prompt through Pro and flash model again. Flash took about a minute and 30 seconds. Pro took about 56 seconds. However, the biggest difference is the number of tokens. Again, Pro generated about 7,700 tokens, whereas this new 3.5 flash generated about 22,000 tokens for the same prompt. Now, this is the output generated by 3.1 Pro preview. The crowd is definitely a lot more aesthetically pleasing compared to what a 3.5 flash is able to generate, but you get very similar quality result. Now, here's another direct comparison between 3.1 Pro and 3.5 flash. This the prompt is actually a very complex prompt that requires a lot of reasoning. So, we're asking it to create a voxel garden scene. Now, if you start going to the chain of thought of both the 3.1 Pro preview and this new 3.5 flash, you see a lot of similarity between how the chain of thought is structured. It feels like Google DeepMind used very similar recipes for training both the Pro and the new 3.5 flash model. And that not only shows in the chain of thought, but also the type of outputs that you get from both of these models. Now, before we look at the output, one thing which I want to show again is the number of tokens that are generated. So, in this case, Gemini 3.1 Pro generated about 20,000 tokens. On the other hand, it generated about 40,000 tokens. So, it's definitely a lot more token hungry and it's going to generate a lot more tokens compared to both the previous flash and Pro versions. Now, in terms of timing, the Pro version took about 2 minutes and 23 seconds. The other one is very close to 2 minutes and 28 seconds. So, the Pro is still a lot slower compared to the flash, but the flash generates a lot more tokens. All right. So, this is the output from Pro. The mist is a bit too high, so you'll have to really zoom in in order to see the structure. Uh but, it's it's pretty good output. Now, this is the output from 3.5 flash. Again, I think it's a very similar quality. You can zoom out. It's a little misty. So, if you zoom out too much, you won't be able to see much. This is the night mode. I think this could definitely use some work. And this is the morning rendering. Now, here's another output for the same prompt for 3.5 flash. These are probabilistic models, so you definitely want to run them a couple of times to see how good the outputs that you can get and how much these prompts are actually followed. I am pretty happy with the type of results that I'm getting out of this model. Now, in terms of web development, the output is very similar to the pro version. Again, for the specific prompt, it took about 4,000 more tokens compared to 3.1 flash or 3.1 pro preview. So, this is the output from 3.1 pro preview, which actually follows the prompt very closely. It also implemented the dark theme, and everything seems to work nicely. Now, for the same prompt, here's the output from 3.5 flash. Again, in terms of the output quality, it looks very similar. So, they definitely use the same sort of recipe that they were using for the pro version. Now, for this next set of prompts, I asked both the models to generate a website for the 25 first legendary Pokémon. This is the output from 3.1 pro preview. Now, for the exact same prompt, here's the output from 3.5 flash. Again, you can see that there is a lot of similarity in the output. Now, given that the pricing is hopefully lower compared to pro, you could definitely see that this will be a valuable model that could give you the 3.1 pro level performance at reduced cost. Okay, so here's another prompt.
