items.title: Minimax Mavis Agent: The Verifier Pattern Changes Everything
items.author: Prompt Engineering
items.summary: The biggest problem with coding agents today is that people usually use the same model for writing code and then verification or debugging it. So, you have the same agent with two different roles.
items.transcriptSummary: The biggest problem with coding agents today is that people usually use the same model for writing code and then verification or debugging it. So, you have the same agent with two different roles.

transcript.text: The biggest problem with coding agents today is that people usually use the same model for writing code and then verification or debugging it. So, you have the same agent with two different roles. The biases that produced the bug in the first place gets preserved when the same model checks its own work. Now, software engineering figured this out a long time ago. Author and reviewers are different people for a reason. And that applies whether the author is human or a model. Just using more agents is not going to fix the problem. If the verified reads the producer's conversation history, it's going to inherit the same framing and biases that made the bug look correct in the first place. For verification to actually catch anything, the verifier has to start from zero. That means no conversation history, no context from the producer, just the artifacts with the specs, and you want to let it do its thing. There is one more problem with coding agents today, and that is they are sequential in nature. The agent works through everything one step at a time. It reads the specs, write the code, runs the test, write the docs, which is sequential by design. But, if you have really good specs out there, you can actually parallelize a lot of these steps. A lot of work doesn't actually depend on the previous step. Reviewing four files for a pull request, maybe translating a document into four different languages, or generating tests across four different modules. None of those needs to wait. You can run them in parallel and get the same work done in a fraction of time. Now, this does not just apply to coding. Most of the knowledge work needs verification. And also, you can have parallel tasks running at the the time. This is this new multi-agent system from MiniMax called Mavis works. And in this video, we're going to look at the different capabilities and I'll walk you through a few really interesting demos where you can not only decompose your task into multiple different subtasks, but you can have an independent verification step that ensures that your agents are not making mistakes. So, let me show you a practical example of this multi-agent system with a separate verification step which can do adversarial reviews. So, this is MiniMax agent. MiniMax is the creator of M2.7 which is arguably one of the best open weight agentic coding model. MiniMax is also the kind sponsor of this video. They recently introduced their token plan that include all modality API subscription for their video, music, image generation, and more. You can get 12% off of their plan with the link in the description. This is a desktop application that you can download and install it on your local machine. And very similar to other desktop application, you can connect it to a folder and the agent is going to have access to it. Now, with this new release, they have included some really interesting new agentic capabilities. So, for example, the first one is that you can create a team of agents. So, in this case, it ships with three agents: coder, verifier, and general. If we look at the setting of this agent, so this is specifically designed for shipping and writing code. Okay? And with every agent, you can actually connect to it and talk to it through an external interface like Telegram, WeChat. So, whenever you are creating a project, then you have the capability to either use a single agent or use a team of agent. And I'll show you whenever you use a team of agent, there is this adversarial verification or review step, which ensures that all the research that is done by the team of agent is actually correct. We'll look at the practical example of that. Now, they also have shipped something called Meow agent, which is kind of an always-on agent or assistant that you can connect to from anywhere. This is going to become your personal assistant that lives on your device. Now, there are also 100 plus skills that are available as a part of this local assistant, or you can bring in your own skills if you want, right? They also have scheduled tasks, so you can schedule tasks for that is going to that are going to repeat or be triggered at a specific time interval of your choosing. Now, let me show you how this adversarial review process in a multi-agent system could look like. So, let me show you an example of how this works in practice. So, in this case, we are providing it to do market research on the state of open weight models today. I'm supposed to create a 15-slide presentation. And we are asking it to do research on architectural differences, price comparisons, and recent benchmarks, and where things are heading, right? Now, I am asking it to do research on these models, which are independent tasks. So, I can use parallel agents to basically perform the analysis independently for every model or do the research, and then combine the results for collective analysis, right? And I'm simply telling it to use the pre-built slide component. Okay, so just like other coding models, we can select the permission level. So, we're going to just bypass everything, so it doesn't have to ask us. And you can also attach files at photos or specific skills. Now, in this case, I think it's going to be able to select the appropriate skill for building the slides. So, we'll just send this in and let's see what happens. Okay, so since we're using a team of agents, so I think it is looking up that specific skill. Now, it's launching four parallel research agents, one per model. And it says that this is a deep research territory. So, the team plan is the right call. So, there is an orchestrator, which looks at the user prompt, and based on that, it decides how many parallel agent it's going to need. And then, it's building these agents, which you can actually see here. So, there is a research agent for M2.7, there's a deep seek one, uh Quen, and for Gemma 4. All right, it actually gives you the visibility. So, right now, you can actually see that it created different sessions for different agents, right? So, not only it created those multiple parallel agents, but you can go and see what exactly each of these agent is doing, which is pretty great. Okay, so something interesting. It says Quen research session crashed at startup. Uh session failed before even picking up the task. Other three sessions may have the same issue. I'm monitoring and will retry once current sessions complete. You can actually see that the Quen one did not run. Uh I think it will be able to fix that. The other ones are running fine. Okay, so while we wait, DeepSeek research agent has completed its task. And you can actually see the research report that it has generated. So here's the technical information, key innovations. So the research phase is done. And then after the research phase, it actually created independent verifiers for all the claims. So this one is for the M2.7. Here it says verification completed. There seems to be an error or an issue that it couldn't verify some of the things, right? So it went back and redid the analysis, which is pretty great, right? Because like every step that you take actually is verified by an independent adversarial review agent. Now the beauty is that it doesn't share the context of your agent that did the research. It knows the objective. And based on the objective, it looks at the research that was done independently and is able to highlight issues that probably are going to be missed if you use same agent for research and then verification with shared context. So by system design, I think it's a really neat idea to have this independence between the two steps. Now once that is done, it created the slide deck for us. I'll show you that in a minute. But this is a really good example of the type of knowledge work you want your agents to do. So imagine instead of this, if it's your company data and you're doing some sort of report generation, you would use a very similar workflow. Okay, so here's the slide deck that it created. And by the way, it also generated this image in the background. So it seems like it has access to an image generation model. This is the table of content, so model overview, benchmark results, pricing, architecture comparison, and where things are heading. Now, it generated multiple different files for each slide. They didn't put together everything in a single slide or HTML file, but that's something we can easily fix. Also, I think if you prompted based on the design system that we want, we are going to get much better results in terms of the visual output. But still, I think it's it's it's pretty good for the first pass, right? Here's kind of the pricing comparison of different models. I'm actually happy that it kind of highlighted that. A MiniMax and 2.7 uses a modified MIT license, and so it is able to capture those nuances along with that Deep Seek before Pro has the 75% discount on pricing at the moment. And in here, this is where key takeaways and the future trends. Now, interestingly enough, it says MOE dominates cost versus performance, then agentic coding is the battleground. Open weight is not equal to free for commercial use, right? So, it gives examples of M2.7 has a modified MIT license, so it's not non-commercial. Self-evolution is the next MOE. Context window hit 1 million context window. Proprietary is not always better. I agree with most of the assessment that it has here. Okay, so it seems like the verify actually found a factual error. And this is why you want to have this independent verification step. So, this is in the pricing of how the report has priced M2.7, uh which is actually a very good catch. Okay, so this was a quick example of how you can use a multi-agent system that executes multiple different agents in parallel, and then the importance of a review with an independent verifier. Now, there is one more interesting thing about this agent, and that is memory. Memory is a big thing right now. Everybody is trying to figure out what is the best way for the agent to preserve information. So, in this case, what you can you can do is you can ask the agent to remember something, or it also automatically remembers the most important information. Now, there are three different types of memories. One is session memory, which basically is in context learning. The second one is agent memory. And this is I think a big thing. The way this works is that agent is going to look at a session and figure out what it has done and what are the most important things that it can write to its memory. So, for example, here in this session it says that the playwrighting screenshot path restricts the team plan interface at three cycles. Okay? So, this is something important that it can reuse, and that's why it wrote it to the agent memory. Then, there is a third level, which is user memory, or let's call it global or permanent memory. So, this is for task across user knowledge. Now, in this case, it wrote to the memory about the pricing. So, I went to another session and I simply asked it, "Do you remember how cheap was M200 compared to Opus?" So, rather than going to the web and try to fetch that information, it is simply looking at its memory. So, it says, "Based on my memory, I recall from the user profile that there was a research session, right?" And then, here's like how cheap it was relative to Opus. And this is important because not only the agent will be able to learn from its mistake, that is self-improvement in a way through its agent memory, but it can also remember things across different sessions for you as a user. And this is becoming a very critical piece that most of these agentic systems still lacks. Now, uh Minimax Agent is a desktop application that uh you can run locally with your local file access. Okay, so here's the web version of the same agent. Now, the desktop application runs locally, has local file access, but this is more for a cloud-hosted solution. And I was actually looking at it, so here they have Max Hermes, Max Claw, which is different than the Mavis Assistant that we saw. These are the Minimax version of Open Claw and Hermes Agent with all the different capabilities, but they are hosting the solution for you. So, it's basically one-click deployment that you can use with the Minimax models with very similar capabilities to what you get out of Open Claw or Hermes Agent. Now, I have personally used the Hermes Agent, and I think it's pretty good, especially its ability to solve goals. So, if you don't want to set it up locally, this is a really good option to get started and get a feel of what the agent is able to do because it lives in the cloud, so you don't even have to worry about setting it up on your local machine. You will need to give it access to the tools uh that you want it to use. Anyways, I do check out Minimax Agent. Make sure to check out their token plan. You get 12% off with this unique link in the video description. Also, do let me know what type of agentic architectures that you have worked with. Have you found self-verification to be useful or it's just an additional step in your experimentation? Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

items.title: The Model Doesn't Matter. The Harness Does.
items.author: Prompt Engineering
items.summary: If you're using one of these coding agents that lets you select models from different providers, you're probably using one model for planning and another for execution. However, that probably is not a good idea. It's called harness design for long-running application development. You're looking at multi-file long-running um long time horizon tasks.
items.transcriptSummary: If you're using one of these coding agents that lets you select models from different providers, you're probably using one model for planning and another for execution. However, that probably is not a good idea. It's called harness design for long-running application development. You're looking at multi-file long-running um long time horizon tasks.

transcript.text: If you're using one of these coding agents that lets you select models from different providers, you're probably using one model for planning and another for execution. However, that probably is not a good idea. Cursor just released this blog post which shows that switching models mid-conversation might actually be hurting the performance of your agent. And coding agents that lets you bring any models may not be giving you the optimal performance for everything. This video, I want to walk you through what they found, why it matters, and what it actually means for the way you pick your tool. Okay, so let me show you what exactly the Cursor team is saying. So, here's a quote. All of our harness abstractions are model agnostic and can be heavily customized for every model we support. For instance, OpenAI's models are trained to edit files using patch-based format, while Anthropic models are trained on string replacement. End quote. And then uh they make the key point. Uh either model could use either tool, but giving it [music] the unfamiliar one costs extra reasoning tokens and produces more mistakes. So, what they are saying here is simple, but the implications are huge. OpenAI's models are trained with patches, kind of like Git diff. Uh Claude models are trained with string replacements, uh which means find this exact text, replace it with the other text. Now, when you give Claude a patch-style tool, you're asking it to operate in a format it does not natively know. And when you give uh GPT models a string replace tool, same problem, but just in reverse. Now, Cursor has the resources to build separate harnesses for every model that they support. They can AB test it. They can tune it for every provider. But, most third-party agents you're using, they probably don't have the resources. There is another problem that every model creators are coming up with their own format. The end result is that most of these harnesses just pick one format and apply it everywhere. And that's why the same model can feel like a genius in one tool and pretty average in another. It's not the model that change, it's the harness around it. Okay, so the customization is just one piece. Cursor's block walk through three other things that you need as a model creator or agent harness creator. Now, the first is dynamic context. Older harnesses loaded everything the model might need up front. But, there is a simple trade-off. If you load too little, the model cannot see what it needs. If you load too much, you're burning tokens that you don't have to burn. Cursor's approach is different. They wrote quote, "We have adopted to increase model's capability by knocking down guardrails and providing more dynamic context, which can be fetched by the agent while it works." End quote. So, the agent can decide what context it needs and when it needs it. The second thing is how they track errors. So, they classify every tool error into three different categories. Invalid arguments, unexpected environments, and provider error. The first two are model mistakes. The third is when the underlying provider actually fails. And here uh are the results in their own words. We're looking at a 10 times reduction for the same model just by the tuning of harness around the model. Most production agents need access to live data from the web. The problem is that web scraping is a pain. You're dealing with CAPTCHAs, rate limits, HTML layouts that change every few weeks, and proxies. All things that have nothing to do with the actual project you're trying to ship. SerpAPI handles all of this for you. You make one API call and you get clean, structured JSON back from Google, Bing, YouTube, and a bunch of others. There's no scraping, no parsing on your end. You just get the data you need. For AI engineering, this fits really well. If you're building an AI agent that needs to look up things, their Google Search API gives you the latest information. Do you need a data set for pre-classified images for training? Their Google Images API gives you the titles, URLs, and thumbnails ready to go. Are you building a research assistant? Their Google Scholar API gives you the full peer-reviewed paper metadata. We're going to uh talk about agents in a few minutes, and a lot of production agent setups use SerpAPI as one of their core tools. You can get started with 250 free credits. Link is going to be in the description or scan the QR code on the screen. Big thanks to SerpAPI for sponsoring this video. And the third is how they measure quality. They call it keep rate. There are two ways. The first is the keep rate of agent-generated code. We track what fraction of those code changes remain in the user's code base after fixed interval of time. So, if you keep the code written by the agents, keep rate goes up. If you as a user delete or rewrite it, keep rate drops. And this is what production grade agent and measurement actually looks like. You're not tracking some vanity benchmarks. The percentage of the agent's work that survive contact with you is what cursor is interested in. Now, harness design is a very hot topic right now. And if you have noticed, I have been creating a lot of videos around it because it's a very interesting engineering problem. Now, cursor is not alone. Anthropic also released a very interesting blog post recently on this exact problem. It's called harness design for long-running application development. The post walks through what they tried first, a single agent. You give it a task and let it work. They call this the solo approach. It ran for 20 minutes. The cost was around $9 and the output barely worked. Then, they built a full harness around the same model. In this case, there were three agents, a planner, a generator, and an evaluator working together. The planner expanded the requests into full product specs. This is basically specs-driven development. The generator worked in sprints, picking up one feature at a time. And the evaluator used the Playwright MCP to actually click through the running app like a real-world user would. Now, this is how you want to divide your projects into multiple different parts, which can be verified. The results in in Tropic's own words, were "The harness was over 20 times more expensive, but the difference in the output quality was immediately apparent." Now, using a complicated harness is going to definitely cost you. But, you're going to get massively better results with the same model but just changing the scaffolding. In a follow-up, run uh they brought it down to 4 hours and $124, which is still expensive. Now, the beauty is that you can actually quantify this. You can run benchmarks uh with different harnesses but same model, and you're going to see dramatically different results. [clears throat] So, one good example of this is SweepBench Pro. Most coding benchmarks let each agent bring its own scaffolding. So, a cursor is one example of that scaffolding, Claude Code is another. SweepBench Pro is different. It forces every model into the same minimal scaffold, uh which is basically a bash-only tool, a fixed turn limit, no custom optimization. So, you can isolate raw model capabilities from harness craft. Now, here is uh what happened when they tested it on SweepBench Pro. These results are for Opus 4.5, uh which is a couple of generation older, but directionally the results are going to hold. So, it scored 80.9% on SweepBench Verified. On SweepBench Pro, uh with the standard minimal scaffolding, the score was 45.9%. Now, to be fair, SweepBench Pro is also a much harder uh set of tasks. You're looking at multi-file long-running um long time horizon tasks. So, part of the gap is task difficulty. But, here is the part that's purely scaffolding. Take the same Opus 4.5 on the Swebench Pro task. Now, let's different agent systems bring their own custom harnesses. Now, on the same benchmark, Cursor harness gets it up to 50.2%. Claude could harness pushes it to 55.4. That's roughly a 10-point swing for the same model, same task, but simply different scaffolding. So, when Cursor and Anthropic tell you they spend their engineering effort on the harness, this is what they mean. The harness is not just a wrapper. It's the actual multiplier these days. Now, if you thought harness matters this much, what happens when you switch models mid-conversation? This is a pattern I have seen a lot. People usually plan with one model, and implementation is done with another model. Cursor walked walks to this in a section literally called facilitating mid-chat model switching. And here's what they say is hard about it. In their words, "Different models have different behaviors, prompts, and tool shapes." End quote. So, when you switch, the new model has to apply its tools to a conversation history produced by a different model, which is out of distribution from what it was trained on. Now, there's another problem. A cache provider and model specific switching means a cache miss. So, your first turn after the switch is going to be slower and more expensive. Now, here's Cursor's solution. They insert custom instructions that warn um the new model. Hey, uh, you just took over from another model. Some of these tools in the chat history are not yours. Don't call them. That's the engineering work needed for a feature that every uh, provider makes look like a single click. And here's the actual recommendation. We generally recommend staying with one model for the duration of a conversation unless you have a reason to switch. End quote. Now, this is not only limited to cursor. Even for something like Claude code, they recommend not switching between Opus, uh, and Sonnet or Haiku mid-conversation. And the reasons are very similar to what a cursor highlighted. Because every, uh, um, drop-down that lets you switch models mid- task is hiding real cost. You're going to have slower turns, uh, cache misses, and a model operating on context that does not fully understand it. It looks free, but it's not. Now, let's talk about the role of agent harnesses in multi-agent [clears throat] systems. So, Cursor, um, in their blog post, called, uh, this, The Harness and the Future of Software Development. Here is how they describe what's coming in their own words. Quote, instead of running every, uh, subtask through a single agent, the system will learn to delegate across specialized agents and sub-agents. One for planning, another one for fast edits, and the third one for debugging. Now, this sounds great in theory. You can break the work down into pieces and let specialized agents handle each piece. It's an easy win. But, here's the math that you actually need to care about. So, take one agent at 95% reliability, which seems pretty good, right? Now, chain five of them together, a planner, an editor, a debugger, a reviewer, and a tester. Each one uh with 95% reliability. However, at the end of the chain, you're going to get to 77.4% reliability. Because the errors are going to accumulate, your end-to-end reliability just collapsed. So, the work that flowed perfectly through one agent now fails one in four times. This is the compounding problem, and it's why multi-agent systems that look great uh in the beginning starts to fall apart in production systems. So, you need to actually rethink on how to build multi-agent systems. Cursor's answer to this is making that work well is fundamentally uh harness challenge, end quote. The system needs to know which agent to dispatch, how to frame the task for each agent's strength, how to stitch the results into a coherent workflow. Now, in this case, the orchestration logic does not live in a single agent. It lives in the harness. And you need to treat harnesses as an ever-evolving software system. While harness engineering has always been important for agent success, it's only going to be more critical going forward. Which, in translation, the harness was a small piece of agent quality in the past, it's about to be the whole game. So, what does any of this means if you're building an agent or using these tools? So, there are three takeaways that I want you to consider. Number one, if you're building agents, stop treating prompts and tools as glue code. You need to version them, measure them, run AB tests, uh track keep rate or whatever your version of it is. Treat the harness like an actual product because it is becoming one. Number two, if you're picking a model, don't trust headline benchmarks. Ask which harness produced the numbers. The same Opus 45 or 47, we showed use code 80% on one benchmark and 46% on another. It's the same model, different harness, the numbers tells you nothing without the context. Number three, the most important one, your moat as a builder is not model access. Everyone has access to exactly the same model. Your moat is harness craft, the orchestration logic, the context strategy, the error handling, the tool format choices for each provider. Two years ago, the harness was a small piece of agent quality. Now, it's the most important agent quality. So, if you're using agents or building a harness around agents, I think you need to pay close attention to model specific tools and harnesses. This is where you're going to get the most gain. So, in 2026, I think it's the wrong question to ask, uh which model is the best. The right question is which harness around a specific model is the best one. If you're building agentic systems, do let me know in the comment section below. If you like the content, uh subscribe to the channel. I am I'm to be putting out a lot of technical content around agent harnesses, sandboxes, system designs. So, if you're interested in content like this, make sure to subscribe to the channel. Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

items.title: RAG is Dead. Again. (Claude Agent SDK + Memory)
items.author: Prompt Engineering
items.summary: You can use Claude for a lot more than coding. In this video, I'm going to show you a setup which gives your agent a multi-layered memory system which you can use for retrieval of information from any documents.
items.transcriptSummary: You can use Claude for a lot more than coding. In this video, I'm going to show you a setup which gives your agent a multi-layered memory system which you can use for retrieval of information from any documents.

transcript.text: You can use Claude for a lot more than coding. In this video, I'm going to show you a setup which gives your agent a multi-layered memory system which you can use for retrieval of information from any documents. This system is built on top of the agent SDK, but you can use it with Claude code. It uses some ideas from my agentic file search open-source project that gives your agent a set of simple tools that helps it to retrieve information from both structured and unstructured documents. To show you how it works, here are 16 different documents, each with a very complex layout that combines free-flowing text, tables, and images. Now, here I'm asking a complex question that needs information retrieval from multiple different documents, and then it needs the agent to reason about its results. So, here is how it works. The agent initially makes a plan, reads the folder, then based on that, it identifies the documents that it thinks are going to be interesting. It passes those files, look at those individually, then it does semantic search, and based on all of that analysis and results, it actually comes up with a response that is well-grounded in the documents that we are providing. And it also has the ability to go back if it missed a specific source during its retrieval process. Now, instead of retrieval of information, you can use this system as a memory system for your agent. At the moment, this agent is powered by Claude agent SDK, but you can use the same setup with Claude code or even Codex if you simply create an MCP server or skill around this thing. Now, the memory system has two different components. The first one is semantic similarity based. For this, we're using Milvus, which is a high-performance vector store. More on that later in the video. And then, the second one is simple tools which can use the file system. These tools involve scanning documents in a folder, reading those documents, parsing the documents, and searching for those documents. Think of this as tools that are available to Claude code when it's doing search for specific code segments. Now, we do one more thing. For every page that has a picture, we take a screenshot of that. All of this parsing of the documents is happening through Light Parser, which is an open-source project from Llama Index. And for vector store, I'm using Milvus, which is a high-performance vector store that can scale with your data. Here's the ingestion pipeline, which is going to look very familiar. It's a standard ingestion pipeline with one key difference. For document parsing, we are using Light Parser, which is great for processing complex structures in PDF files. And the idea is that not only it can process text, but you can detect the presence of images, and for those, we actually take screenshots and store them separately. Now, this is helpful because you don't have to take a screenshot of every page. You only take screenshot of the pages that actually have visual content in it, like images or graphs. But you also keep all the text components, which are going to be critical for text-based retrieval. Then, we run through a simple chunking process. In this case, we are using the Gemini embedding as a high-dimensional embedding, and everything is stored in Milvus vector store. Now, let's talk about the data structure that we are saving. Now, here's the simple schema of everything that we are storing inside Milvus. It's great. It can scale up to billions of records. But right now, we are including the source document, the extracted text, the computing computed embedding, then path to the actual image files that are extracted from it, and then also a number of different key metadata that we use for metadata-based filtering. Okay, so why Milvus? This is a full vector database, which is built for scale. It supports both CPU and GPU acceleration, which makes it a very interesting choice. If you're using Milvus light, you can just create a single file that contains everything. But if you need scale, this supports fully distributed Kubernetes native architecture that enables it to scale horizontally, which means you can run tens of thousands of search queries on billions of vectors. And the best part, it's under Apache 2.0, so you can use it for commercial purposes. And it has over 40,000 stars on GitHub. But if you want a hosted solution, they have Zilliz Cloud, which runs Milvus as a fully managed service for you. It's very simple to use, so you'll first create the client, then define the collection name, what is going to be the dimensions of your vector store, and after that, just provide your data for data ingestion. They are also the current sponsors of today's video. Now, the agent itself has two sets of tools. One is powered by Milvus. This is basic semantic similarity web search, and also the agent can get images, which are the screenshots of pages that contains visual information. On the other hand, it has access to file system. Basically, a number of different bash tools which enables the agent to scan a folder, preview different documents, parse them, read the entire document, and look for patterns. These tools are extremely powerful if you're doing any sort of search. Now, reading every document through file system-based tools is going to be pretty expensive. So, that's why we use the indexed path as a pre-filtering. So, we use the semantic similarity search to reduce the search space, to identify the most relevant chunks, and then we use these file system-based tools to read through the documents to which these chunks belong. And in the process, the system is smart enough that it can go back and look at a specific document if it was missed during the initial retrieval process. Now, here's the retrieval pipeline. The user asks a question, we compute embeddings, Milvus does the cosine similarity-based retrieval, we get top chunks, and then Claude through the agent SDK can look at those chunks, reason through it, can find the documents from which these chunks are coming from, read through those documents if it needs to, and backtrack if it thinks that the initial semantic retrieval missed any documents that are relevant. So, here's the GitHub repo. The code is going to be available for you to experiment. Now, this is going to give you an overview of what exactly the different tools are. Here's the main strategy that I tried to explain. Initially, it does a parallel scan, then deep dive, and then backtrack if it needs further information. Also, here are the instructions on how to set it up. I have already set up everything. So, I'm going to just walk you through a few examples of how this system works. Okay, so let me show you some example of how this system works, and it can do long horizon search. Now, for this example, we're using medical data that I have collected from multiple different sources. So, here's from FDA on how to manage high blood pressure. If you look at these documents, they have free-flowing text, tables, images, and there's no single structure, which it makes it very complex. If you use something like DocLing, it is going to have pretty hard time parsing this. So, this is where Llama Parser comes into play. One of the things that it does pretty nicely is that not only it can extract data from tables, but also it can identify images, and then take screenshot of these pages and put them separately. Now, this first question question is about the side effect of a specific medicine. This is something that an agentic rag or retrieval augmented generation system would be able to easily do because if you're using semantic similarity, then it can just look at what exactly this means, and will probably be able to find you the document in the first pass. And this is kind of what happens here as well. Initially, it is thinking about a plan, then runs the query through the semantic search mechanism, and it thinks that, okay, there's this one specific table. It fetches that and after that, it runs another search query to ensure that it has all the information and it can generate the response pretty accurately, which is grounded in the documents that it was looking at. But then, you can run more complex queries. So, let's say if you run a query like compare the diabetes medication guides from FDA versus ADA and what they cover differently. This is something that a normal rag system might have struggled with unless you run it through query decomposition, which initially looks at one guide, then the second one guide, and then have another reasoning step through it to look at the second one. Now, since we mentioned specific guides, so it actually goes scan the documents or scan the folders, identify that specific file, pick up both of these, and then go through a reasoning loop to produce the final answer. Then, you can have more open-ended questions. So, what food interactions should patient on the blood pressure medication be aware of? Now, in this case, the agent decides to have multiple different passes, and you can actually see that for every search query, it keeps changing it based on the context that it's gathering to ensure that it captures a wide variety of information sources. But, there is more you can do here. Uh it has access to a specific set of tools. You can tell the agent to use those tools. Uh so, let's say in this case we're asking for it to look for blood thinners and then scan the document folder to find any additional sources that were not covered in the initial search results. And the process that you see here is that initially it does search, then based on the queries that it generated, it starts parsing different documents and starts reading through them. Now, this one takes about 16 different steps. However, this kind of ensures that it's looking at all the relevant documents and making sure that whatever were missed during the initial retrieval, it's able to capture that. And that's kind of the beauty of this system. You can run pretty complex queries through it. So, this was a quick overview of the project that I'm currently building. I've given Claude agent's persistent memory. The code is open source. A link to the GitHub repo is going to be in the video description. I hope you find this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

panel function this panel will contain the chat dialogue that we can use to chat with deep seek now this panel contains a web view that we can customize with regular HTML I'm going to go ahead and Define that HTML in its own separate function called get webview content and the UI is really no different than a basic web page we have a text area and a button the interesting part comes into play in the script tag but one thing that's making this video suck right now is that there's no syntax highlighting on the code because I'm writing this HTML in a string literal in a Javascript file well we can actually fix that with a vs code extension es6 string HTML allows us to use this HTML comment on a string literal to get syntax highlighting directly in a Javascript file and now we can look more closely at the JavaScript code inside this HTML code inside this JavaScript code inside the vs code editor which itself is built with JavaScript where we first connect to the vs code API which then allows us to post messages back and forth with the chat the first thing that will happen is the user will type a prompt into the text box begging our Chinese AI gods for help to capture that event we'll listen to a click on the ask button which will get the text content from the form and then send it over to vs code with this post message method and then identify it with the name of chat that's how we pass a message to the web view now we can go back to our activate function and listen to it on the web view let's define a call back for on did receive message if the message command equals chat then we know we have a user prompt and we can pass that message to olama in this case it will specify deep seek R1 as the model it pass it the message and set stream to True by streaming the response it'll give us the text sentence by sentence instead of waiting for the entire thing to display that stream in the UI we'll use four weight to Loop over it asynchronously and add the message content to the response text now every time we get a new part of the stream we'll also want to post a message back to the UI which allows us to display the response from the AI to the end user and that means the final step is to listen to this chat response message in the actual UI for that we'll go back to our script tag and add an event listener for message that will take the text from the chat response and set it as the inner text on a div in the actual HTML congratulations you just built your own chines AI powered IDE let's go ahead and test it out by running the debugger that'll bring up the other VSS code window then we can open the command pallet and run the command which in turn will bring up this new window where you can chat when you prompt it it should then stream the response from Deep seek in this div I'm using the 7B model here and when I ask it an easy question like binary search it provides an acceptable solution and as a bonus it's extremely fast but it just goes to show you how easy this stuff is to implement and in fact you could have deep seek build you your own deep seek powered IDE from scratch but if you want to go deeper and get really good at programming while supporting my work consider upgrading to a pro membership at fireship iio you'll get access to all kinds of different Project based courses but most importantly you'll build a foundation to understand what the hell the Chinese AI gods are actually doing when they write your code for you thank for watching and I will see you in the next one

items.title: Is Next.js 15 any good? "use cache" API first look
items.author: Beyond Fireship
items.summary: there are only two hard problems in computer science naming things cash invalidation and off by one errors maybe you've heard that joke before because another problem with programming is that we only have one joke and it's not very funny but there is some truth in the original joke and that's the fact that caching can be extremely difficult and the nextjs JavaScript framework is the poster child for these modern challenges a few weeks ago forcell released nextjs 15 and it comes with all kinds of awesome new features like tools to automatically migrate code a fast turbo pack Dev server and a new API to run code in the background after a request is complete but the biggest change is that they totally overhaul the way caching works and to be honest it's pretty awesome and that's what we'll look at in this video by default everything is cached in xjs like a server component that defines a web page and also any calls you make to the fetch function would be cached by default to try to optimize performance by preventing the server from doing extra work and rendering the same page over and over again the problem is that in some cases you might have multiple data fetching operations to render a page you might want some of them to be cashed but you might want others to be revalidated on every request and opting out of the cash could be pretty annoying there's a few different ways to do it like adding the Cache no store option to fetch or by exporting this Force Dynamic variable or by setting the cach control headers manually it's kind of a mess and easy to run into problems where your data is not updating because it's being cached and you have no idea why the good news is that in X15 things are becoming far more intuitive with a new API called Dynamic IO which allows you to cash or not cash anything while also simplifying your code and making it far more explicit to demonstrate how this works let's look at some code I'm here in a brand new nextjs project but currently this feature is experimental so let's enable it by adding Dynamic IO to the next config and as of today we'll also need to be on the nextjs canary version which you can update to with these commands and optionally you'll notice that I'm using Dino here instead of nodejs because they have some cool utilities that we'll look at later but that's totally optional if you want to learn more about you know my full course is now available and I also just updated my nextjs course and if you're watching this before December 1st 2024 you can use the Black Friday discount to get access to everything at 40% off but now let's take a look at the new caching system in nextjs let's imagine we have an application that needs to display a product description and price to simulate that I've created this utility here that has a separate function for get product and get price I'm using the library Faker JS to generate some fake data which you can think of as data you would fetch from a database interesting backstory on Faker JS a few years ago the original developer intentionally corrupted this project to break a bunch of people's apps on purpose as a statement against open source abuse and lack of funding it actually made it into the mainstream news and was the story that motivated me to start the code report series on the main Channel ultimately it didn't work out too well for him and they took over the project and now it's maintained by someone else in this demo Faker JS is really useful because it makes it easy to tell which data is cached and which is not now from there I'm creating a page on the store Route inside of which we have a server component inside that component we get the product and price synchronously and then render it in the HTML if we go ahead and serve the application you'll notice in the browser that every time I refresh the page I'm getting a new product price in description that's because currently this page is not cach and nextjs will render every time we refresh but now let's make this a little more realistic by going back into our database file then make our data fetching functions async to make it even more realistic I'll bring in this delay function from Dino to Prov a 1second delay to simulate latency in the database then from there I'll go back into the product page and make this server component async and add a weight to our function calls and now if we go back into the browser you'll notice that it renders but we also get this error what this error is telling us is that we need to make a choice we either need to cach or pre-render this page or we need to make it Dynamic explicitly by wrapping it in a suspense boundary let's first assume that we want this page to be dynamic and refetch the data on every request to clear the error the first thing need to do is wrap it in suspense and we can do that easily in nextjs by simply creating a loading file that exports a component this is basically the equivalent of using suspense in the parent layout but now if we go back to the application you'll notice that the error is cleared and we get fresh data on every request but now let's assume that we don't want Dynamic data and would rather cach our data well we can easily accomplish that by simply adding the used cache directive to the top of this page use cache is a nextjs specific feature and not directly related to reactjs s but what you'll notice is that when we go to the browser now we get fresh data on the first render but then get that same data every time we refresh the page and that will continue until the cach expires and that's a good question when does the cach expire well currently the default is an infinite cache that's revalidated every 15 minutes which you'd want for a Content that doesn't change on a regular basis like your about page but what if we want to change the caching Behavior well there's a new cach life function that we can import from nextjs which we can then use in inside the body of the function to change the caching Behavior to a variety of different defaults like in this case we might only want to cach it for a few hours if the product description or price has the potential to change on a daily basis and if these defaults aren't good enough for you you can fully customize them to your own values in the next config but if that's still not good enough for you you can also invalidate the cash on demand which is made possible with this cash tag function you provide the function with a key that's used to identify a cached value and that means that at some point maybe in a couple minutes or a couple years you can invalidate the cache when the underlying data changes like typically that would happen when you update a record in the database that would impact what's presented to the user in the UI pretty cool although at this point we've only looked at ways to Cache an individual route but this Ed cache directive provides us with far more flexibility than that but for example we might have multiple components in a single file and if we put Ed cache at the top of that file all the components will inherit the same behavior if that's not desired though we can move the directive inside of individual components to choose exactly which ones we want to opt in the most powerful feature though is its ability to Cache individual functions and server actions if we go back into the db. TS file we can actually Implement different caching behavior for each function maybe our product description is static and almost never changes is so let's put the use cache directive there in contrast the price might be highly Dynamic so let's leave that one as is now let's go back to our component and figure out how to use multiple levels of caching on a single page first make sure to remove the used cache directive from the top then we'll extract the price UI and data fetching logic into its own component this component will fetch and render the price in the UI just like before but the key difference is that we wrap that component in suspense in the parent what this will do is allow the rest of the page to render immediately while only the price remains suspended in a loading State and that's awesome because otherwise we would have to wait for the price before rendering anything on the page but now as you can see in the demo the user gets most of the content like the product description while the price finishes rendering in the background and that's especially powerful on things like dashboards where you're loading multiple different data sources all of which have different degrees of latency congratulations now that you understand use cach and suspense that's everything you'll need to memorize for caching in next1 15 it's usually a good thing when an API decreases in complexity but as of today Dynamic IO is still experimental and caching is still hard but I think this is a step in the right direction however in a big project this code mik it kind of crazy like your codebase will have used client use server and now use cache everywhere then you might implement the new using keyword in JavaScript to use the new react use hook then combine them all to create your own use using directives and hooks at which point your code will be utterly useless but one other cool thing about next 15 is that it now also supports react 19 and the compiler the react compiler which eliminates the need for annoying hooks like use memo and use callback it's another step in the right direction but is still very experimental today but most importantly these featur are designed to provide job security by giving react developers a never-ending supply of Legacy code to update if react 19 ever comes out someday expect a full course on fireship iio and when that day comes you'll already have access to it if you're a lifetime member thanks for watching and I will see you in the next one

items.title: JavaScript performance is weird... Write scientifically faster code with benchmarking
items.author: Beyond Fireship
items.summary: what you're looking at here are the five rules of programming written by the legendary programmer Rob Pike who programmed the go programming language the first rule of programming is do not talk about programming the second rule of programming is to measure don't tune for Speed until you've measured and even then don't unless one part of the code overwhelms the rest this Echoes another legendary programmer Donald canth who offered similar advice when he said premature optimization is the root of all evil even my dad would tell me measure twice cut once in programming there's a nearly Unlimited a number of ways to skin a cat but the way you write your code matters like as we'll see in JavaScript there's at least four different ways to Loop over an array and count its values but the performance of each technique varies dramatically in today's video I'll show you how to easily Benchmark your JavaScript code so you can scientifically measure its performance and avoid wasting time optimizing red herrings to help us make benchmarking extremely easy we're going to use the JavaScript runtime Dino which has a built-in benchmarking tool after playing around with it for a few days I was able to achieve some amazing things like I figured out how to make array includes 100,000 times faster I was able to write a sorting algorithm that outperforms the built-in array sort method in JavaScript and I figured out the fastest way to write a loop let's take a look at how Doo bench works at first you'll want to make sure you have Doo 2 installed and I also want to point out that this video is part of my full Dino course which is now officially in production and should be out around November 7th which will include about 25 videos and multiple projects the other thing I want to point out is that we're measuring JavaScript and its performance only on the doo runtime which under the hood is powered by the V8 engine which also Powers all chromium browsers and no. JS however if you're looking to compare performance between different run times like node Dino and Bun I'd highly recommend this video from Anton Putra which puts all three of them to the test in a large scale Cloud environment but now let's jump into the code and answer the question what is the fastest way to Loop over an array in JavaScript well I know of at least four different ways to do it which I've represented in these four functions where the goal is to Loop over an array and then sum up the numbers in that array first up we've got the array for each method if you have an array one option is to use for each provide it with a callback function that mutates a variable that works but there is a cleaner way to do this with array reduce which also takes a callback function but also has an accumulated value is so there's no need to mutate that variable you'll see functional declarative code like this a lot in front-end projects like reactjs however the next option is to increment the sum using a regular four Loop in JavaScript you can create a four of Loop that will automatically iterate to the end of the array that's nice and readable but the traditional way to write a for Loop is to use this more explicit syntax where we have a variable of I that's incremented as we Loop over the array's length and that gives us four ways to solve the same problem but the big question is which one will perform the best well the first thing we'll do is create some testing data like in this case generate a large array with 100,000 elements in it and now finally this is where Doo bench comes in on the global Dino namespace you can call the bench method and then either provide it with a regular function to Benchmark or provide it with an object so you can give each Benchmark a name and then the FN property will take a function which is the actual code that you want to run and measure in this file we'll use doench to set up a benchmark for each one of our functions it also helps to set up a baseline which would typically be your original code and in this case I'm going to set that as our traditional for Loop in Doo these benchmarks don't actually affect your runtime code but they act more like testing code and the way to execute the benchmarks is to open up the terminal and run the doench command followed by the file name it'll take a few seconds to run but it gives us this report that shows how many iterations of the function it was able to run per second and its average execution time as well as the best case and worst case performance but at a high level the most useful thing is just the summary that shows us which function is fastest and surprisingly the traditional for Loop is significantly faster than the others and you might be wondering why is that well the main reason is that it doesn't have abstraction or function call Overhead like the four of loop uses the iterator protocol to achieve that syntactic sugar and array for each and reduce require a function call after each iteration that a traditional for Loop doesn't have but the input data matters like if we bump up the number of elements in the array to a million you can see that the benefits of the traditional for Loop increase significantly and now we're getting a 5x performance boost or more but here's the weird part if we reduce the array's length down to about 100 the performance becomes almost even and actually array reduce beats the traditional for Loop so I think the main takeway way here is that the type of loop you use doesn't really matter unless you're looping over massive arrays in which case you'll want to use a traditional for Loop pretty interesting but now I want to show you an optimization that can have a massive impact on performance when searching for a value in an array so in this function I'm generating some testing data that creates a massive array containing product IDs user IDs and emails but then I created another array with 1,000 values which contain some matching values that we want to see if we can find in the big array in vanilla JavaScript the typical way to do this is to use array includes which is a function that will Loop over the array until it finds a matching value and then return a Boolean of true or false in Big O that would give us o of n performance but there's an alternative and more clever way to solve this problem we can take the original array and create a set data structure a set will index everything by unique value and has a method called has that will tell us if that value exists in the set pretty simple but now let's run our Benchmark this time the performance difference is staggering array includes was only able to solve the problem 4.9 times per second while set lookup was able to do the same thing 4.5 million times per second or in other words a million times faster the reason it's so dramatic is because set has o of one performance the unique values are already indexed so it doesn't need to Loop over the entire array it's the same basic concept as creating an index in a relational database now if you're only using array includes on a small number of elements it's probably not worth it to create a set because there is some overhead in creating that index but if you have a large data set it's definitely worth it but now let's talk about sorting arrays there are many ways to do this and I even have a video that compares 10 different sorting algorithms visually but JavaScript also has a built-in array sort method and I was curious to see if I could beat it by implementing three different sorting algorithms like bubble sort quick sort and merge sort I'm not going to go over the implementation in detail but bubble sort is generally considered the simplest algorithm but not very performant while quicksort is a divide and conquer algorithm that is generally considered to be the fastest sorting algorithm for most use [Music] cases and then merge sort which is also a divide and conquer algorithm that can perform really well on certain data structures in this Benchmark I'm going to sort an array of 10,000 elements we'll use the JavaScript sort function as our Baseline and when we run this Benchmark we get some pretty interesting results bubble sort obviously sucks big time merge sort has pretty similar performance but quick sort is almost three times faster that's pretty impressive but why is that well part of it has to do with the overhead of function calls and sort but it also really depends on the thing that you're actually sorting like in this case quicksort is able to do a really good job with numbers but let's see what happens if we change the actual test data to an array of strings when we run the same Benchmark but with strings quick sort is still faster but only just a little bit the bottom line is that in JavaScript sort is probably fast enough for most use cases and the fourth rule of programming is that fancy algorithms are buggier than simple ones and they're much harder to Implement use Simple algorithms as well as simple data structures but if you're really looking to optimize a sorting algorithm you probably shouldn't be messing around with JavaScript and instead use a systems language like C or rust Doo itself is actually written in Rust while the bun JavaScript runtime is written in Zig and nodejs and C++ pretty interesting but I think the main thing I learned through all this benchmarking is that virtually all the built-in stuff is good enough benchmarking is fun but it's far more important to get your code working first and that brings us back to the first rule of programming you can't tell where a program is going to spend its time bottlenecks occur in surprising places so don't try to second guess and put in a speed hack until you've proven that's where the bottleneck is and the way you prove that is by measuring with tools like Dino bench make sure to check out the full Dino course when it comes out next week and here's a discount code for making it to the end of this video thanks for watching and I will see you in the next one

items.title: Does Deno 2 really uncomplicate JavaScript?
items.author: Beyond Fireship
items.summary: last week the highly anticipated dino2 JavaScript runtime was finally released and they launched it with what is easily the greatest commercial for a JavaScript tool ever made it's funny it's packed with Easter eggs and it hits especially hard if you know the pain of JavaScript development the video has nearly a million views on Twitter but I don't want to spoil it in this video we'll use dino2 to build a rest API and find out if it lives up to its bold claim of uncomplicating JavaScript by comparing it to node.js and its other rival bun who had threw some subtle shade out with this other Easter egg yeah I'm sure 99.9% of people missed it but it's a brutal roast a bun which cannot fully handle the nextjs app router without no JS is some people say using JavaScript on the server was the greatest mistake ever made by mankind but I disagree JavaScript is actually one of the fastest soy based languages on the back end and its non-blocking event driven architecture generally performs better than languages like python or PHP it won't beat C++ or rust but it's far easier to use than those languages and Dino addresses almost all the problems that have plagued nodejs development nodejs is an awesome tool as well but the ecosystem suffers from bloat to build something you generally have to mix and match a bunch of different tools and those tools are great but it creates a lot of mental boilerplate and Chaos that you just don't have in dino by the end of this video you'll be able to wipe about a dozen different JavaScript tools from your memory forever before we get started though I have a funny story I actually took a rare vacation back in September to the beautiful country of Croatia before leaving Dino provided me with Early Access to triy out version 2 they didn't sponsor or pay me but I made a code report and scheduled it in advance which is something I almost never do the expected release for Dino 2 was supposed to be late September however they changed the release date while I was out in the middle of nowhere without Wi-Fi then my video went out as scheduled and totally screwed up their release plan Ryan dll the creator of node.js and Dino had to get on Twitter and do some damage control I hope they're not big mad because I really do like Dino and I'm even considering making a full course about it for fireship pro members if that's something you want to see let me know in the comments because as an elderly developer like myself who's over the age of 24 keeping life simple is the only way to maintain my sanity let's jump into some code and see how Dino does that you'll notice that I currently have Dino version 2.0 installed and one tool you can remove from your brain right away is node version manager because Doo can upgrade itself with the upgrade command where you specify the version you want to use unlike nbm though you can't switch between different versions but in Dino that's typically not necessary because of the way things work in its standard library but more on that later but from there we'll create a new project with Dino and nit that creates a project with a main.ts file in it where everything is already configured with typescript and that means you can remove the TS config from your brain next now you can go into the doo. Json file and customize the typescript compiler there but at least you don't have to spend 2 hours failing to configure it just to get started now you'll also notice a main test file Dino has a built-in test framework in runner which we can use immediately by running Dino test and that means we can remove J Jasmine mocha vest chai and virtually any other JavaScript testing library from our brain space now to be fair no Js also released its own test runner in version 20 but I write perfect code that doesn't need to be tested anyway so it's a moot point let's go ahead and write some perfect code now in the main TS file what we want to build is a rest API for horse tender where you can create a horse in a sqlite database then fetch all the horses or just one individual Horse by its ID we could do that with the node.js HTTP module but 99% of the time people will just use expressjs or pick from one of the other 5,000 different web Frameworks the cool thing about doo2 is that you can still use all of those Frameworks because it now supports everything in npm which was not the case in Dino 1 in fact I'm using hono and Dino to build my own little side project right now but another approach is to just use dinos serve which is an HTTP server that's built into the global runtime one thing that's really nice about it is that it uses standard web apis like request response and fetch that means if you're a web developer you can use abstractions you already know and just release all your memories for node.js specific abstractions now it takes a call back function as an argument and let's go ahead and return a basic response to test it out we can now run this code by opening the terminal and hitting Doo run one thing you'll notice is that it requires us to explicitly Grant permission to different features on the system that can improve security and production and it makes it more difficult for hacked npm packages to mine Bitcoin on your system but in development let's go ahead and bypass it with a capital a flag and then I'm also adding a watch flag on here as well to restart the server whenever our code changes and that eliminates yet another tool from our memory nodemon or no demon which needs to be installed in no.
